src/browser/tests/element/html/script.description: A collection of HTML and JS test assets that validate <script> element behaviors (sync/async/defer), dynamic script insertion, load event semantics, and text extraction for the browser runtime. These files are static test fixtures used by the project's browser test harness and exercise interactions among the network loader, HTML parser/DOM, script execution engine, and event dispatch system.
key_behaviors: Static HTML pages load inline and remote <script> elements with different attributes (async, defer, module) and assert execution order and DOM readiness.
Dynamic creation/insertion of <script> elements (document.createElement/appendChild/innerHTML/TextContent) triggers network fetch of .js resources and observable load/onload event sequences.

Remote .js fixtures (dynamic1.js, dynamic2.js, order*.js, empty.js) are fetched and executed; their side effects (incrementing counters, appending to a list, calling test harness APIs) drive expectations.
Tests assert text extraction and parsing behaviors for script element contents (e.g. forcing async, handling empty scripts, preserving text nodes).
Test harness globals (expectEqual, testing) and event listeners validate results after script execution and DOM events.

insights.title: Tests rely on deterministic ordering and timing of script execution and onload events
insights.problem: Async/defer/dynamic insertion tests assume specific relative ordering between script execution, DOM parsing, and load event delivery.
insights.root_cause: Script execution ordering depends on multiple subsystems (network loader, parser scheduling, script-manager/V8 execution) and environment timing; small changes in scheduling or network latency can break expectations.
insights.solution: Treat these tests as timing-sensitive: any change that affects scheduling, network fetch ordering, or script evaluation timing must preserve the original execution semantics or update the test expectations.

insights.title: JS fixtures are referenced by exact filenames and served as static assets
insights.problem: Renaming or moving .js/.html files will break references within tests and any server/static asset mapping used by test runner.
insights.root_cause: HTML fixtures use hardcoded URLs/paths to dynamic1.js, dynamic2.js, order.js, etc.; the test runner serves these files directly.
insights.solution: When changing file names or directories, update all HTML references and the test asset serving configuration; keep relative paths stable when running local or CI tests.

insights.title: Tests depend on global test harness APIs and specific event wiring
insights.problem: Fixtures call global helpers like expectEqual and testing; they also rely on adding listeners (document.documentElement.addEventListener) to observe events.
insights.root_cause: The test assets are small and delegate assertions to the external harness rather than self-contained verification.
insights.solution: Any change that alters how test harness functions are provided, or modifies event propagation semantics, requires coordinating with the harness or adapting the fixtures' assumptions.

insights.title: Dynamic/remote script tests assume local HTTP serving and same-origin behavior
insights.problem: Tests expect remote scripts to be fetched from the same origin and that the test environment will serve .js files without CORS or caching surprises.
insights.root_cause: The fixtures are not embedded; they require the project test server or CI worker to expose these assets over HTTP.
insights.solution: Ensure the test server serves these assets at the expected paths, honors cache-control (or disables caching) for consistent behavior, and that any proxying or preflight/CORS changes do not interfere with expected loads.

key_files.why: Central test page that composes inline and remote scripts to verify overall execution ordering, defer/async semantics, and ties to order*.js expectations.
Primary JS fixture that records/validates load/execution order for the order.html test harness.
Exercises dynamic script creation and appendChild behavior; relies on dynamic1.js and dynamic2.js being fetched and executed.
Remote JS fixture used by dynamic.html as part of the dynamic-load execution ordering and side-effect checks.
Validates text extraction and async handling for script element content — touches parsing and text node handling semantics.

construct_relationships: order.html references order.js, order_async.js, and order_defer.js to assert the relative execution order between inline, remote, async, and defer scripts.
dynamic.html programmatically creates <script> elements that fetch dynamic1.js and dynamic2.js; those .js files mutate globals checked by the test harness.
dynamic_inline.html tests multiple insertion approaches (TextContent, innerHTML, src attribute) to trigger script evaluation pathways in the parser/execution pipeline.
script.html attaches document.documentElement.addEventListener and script.onload handlers to observe load events emitted by the loader and DOM event system.
async_text.html and empty.js together validate how empty or async-marked script elements are parsed and how their textContent is preserved or ignored.
notable_relationships: These test assets are upstream of core browser subsystems: ScriptManager (V8 execution), network/http (fetching remote .js), and html5ever-based parser (building DOM and scheduling script parsing).
The test harness globals used by the fixtures are provided externally by the browser's test runner; mismatches between harness API and fixtures will break assertions.
CI and end-to-end tests rely on a local HTTP server serving these files; changes to build/serve logic (e.g., static asset paths, caching, precompiled snapshots) can affect these tests.
Because the fixtures validate timing and ordering, changes to event dispatch, task scheduling, or network concurrency in other subsystems have direct impact on these tests.
recommendations: Do not rename, move, or change public URLs of these test files without updating all HTML references and the test asset server configuration.

When editing any .html or .js fixture, preserve the observable side effects (global variables, list mutations, event emissions) relied on by the assertions or update the corresponding assertion fixtures in lockstep.

Respect script element semantics (async, defer, module vs classic) — modifications that alter attribute handling or scheduling must be validated by running the test suite (zig build test and project end-to-end tests).

Before committing changes that affect script execution, run unit tests and the browser end-to-end tests (make test and make end2end if applicable) in both local and CI-like environments to capture timing-sensitive regressions.
Ensure the test server used in local development and CI serves these fixtures with the same paths, headers (including cache behavior), and origin assumptions the tests expect.

src/browser/tests/element/html.description: This folder contains HTML test fixtures that exercise intrinsic HTML element behaviors and their integration with the DOM, event dispatch, and form/selection semantics. The files are static pages used by the browser test harness to assert attribute↔property reflection, default values, input/click behaviors, form association, and element-specific state (open/selected/disabled). These tests are sensitive to the parser, event ordering, and JS execution environment.
key_behaviors: Attribute ↔ property reflection and default-value initialization across many element types (e.g., open, disabled, name, value, defaultSelected, tabIndex, hidden).
Form association and submission semantics: elements discovering their form via ancestor vs form attribute, submitter semantics, and element participation in form submission.
User-interaction flows for form controls: click event dispatch, preventDefault interaction with state changes (checkbox/radio), selection and focus-affecting behaviors.
Grouping and name-based linkage for radios/labels: group formation, case-sensitivity, and cross-form grouping rules.
Open/returnValue state propagation for interactive container elements (details, dialog) and reflection to attributes.

insights.title: Missing requestSubmit(submitter) semantics tests in form fixtures
insights.problem: Form tests note the absence of coverage for requestSubmit(submitter) and default submitter behavior.
insights.root_cause: The fixture set includes form behavior checks but lacks explicit scenarios for programmatic submission via requestSubmit with a provided submitter element.
insights.solution: Treat any code changes affecting form submission discovery, submitter selection, or requestSubmit behavior as potentially breaking for tests; add or update tests if semantics change and ensure form submission code preserves backward-compatible submitter handling.

insights.title: Many fixtures assert tight attribute↔property reflection
insights.problem: Tests assume properties reflect to attributes and vice versa (e.g., open, disabled, name, value, defaultSelected), including initial default values.
insights.root_cause: HTML element implementations must initialize default property values and keep attribute changes synchronized with property values per spec.
insights.solution: When editing element initialization or attribute mutation code, ensure reflection rules and default initialization remain consistent with expectations expressed in these fixtures; changes here will cascade to many tests that rely on initial states.

insights.title: Click/preventDefault interactions for checkboxes and radios are sensitive
insights.problem: Fixtures test that preventDefault can revert state changes, observe event order, and that clicks on already-checked radios may be suppressed.
insights.root_cause: The DOM event dispatch pipeline and synthetic activation behavior must match the precise ordering and default-prevention semantics the tests expect.
insights.solution: Any modifications to click handling, activation behavior, or event propagation must preserve the ordering and preventDefault semantics; otherwise, tests for toggling, event sequences, and state visibility inside handlers will fail.

insights.title: Radio grouping and form association semantics are exercised extensively
insights.problem: Tests encode nuanced behavior: radios without name should not group, form attributes influence grouping across tree boundaries, and name comparison may be case-sensitive.
insights.root_cause: Correct implementation requires following grouping rules (name + tree/form context) and case sensitivity rules defined by the DOM/HTML specs.
insights.solution: Ensure changes that touch name resolution, grouping algorithms, or form association logic account for cross-boundary grouping and case-sensitivity expectations to avoid breaking these fixtures.

insights.title: Disabled state propagation and its reflection to attributes is tested across elements
insights.problem: Multiple fixtures expect disabled booleans to initialize and reflect to attributes and to influence interactive behavior (e.g., preventing clicks).
insights.root_cause: Disabledness affects event handling and activation; tests assert both attribute presence and behavioral consequences.
insights.solution: Preserve the semantics where disabled attributes change behavior (suppress activation/clicks) and reflect to properties; any change in disabled handling is likely to require corresponding test updates.

key_files.why: Central form semantics fixture; explicitly notes missing coverage for requestSubmit and drives form-related assertions used across many control tests.
Encodes click activation, preventDefault, and event-order invariants for checkboxes and radios — sensitive to event dispatch and activation logic.
Exercises radio grouping, name/form interactions, and case-sensitivity — touches grouping algorithm and form association implementation.
Verifies dialog open/returnValue state reflection and initialization; changes to dialog state handling will impact these expectations.
Checks option value/text/selection/defaultSelected behaviors that interact with select/option selection model and initial value logic.

construct_relationships: Attribute↔Property: Many tests (open, disabled, name, value, defaultSelected, tabIndex, hidden) assert that attribute mutations and property changes are reflected bidirectionally and initialized to correct defaults.

Event dispatch ↔ activation: Click events on input elements are expected to follow a deterministic ordering where event handlers can observe interim state and preventDefault can revert activation changes.

Form association ↔ element discovery: Form-associated elements either locate their form via ancestor traversal or a form attribute; requestSubmit and normal submission flows depend on correct association.
Radio grouping ↔ name resolution: Radios group based on name and form context; grouping logic must consult both the DOM tree structure and the form attribute to compute mutual exclusivity.
Selection model ↔ option/selected: Option elements' value/text/defaultSelected interplay with the select element's selection model and initial setup routines.
notable_relationships: These fixtures exercise the HTML parser and DOM construction (src/html5ever/*) because they rely on initial attribute states and tree structures produced at parse time.
Event-order and activation tests rely on the event dispatch system and input activation paths implemented in the runtime (Page/ScriptManager and event manager modules).

Form submission and requestSubmit semantics interact with network and navigation code paths (form submit leading to HTTP requests handled by src/network/http.zig) and possibly ScriptManager if scripts call submit APIs.
Radio/label/form behaviors may be affected by changes in the element lifecycle or tree mutation behavior implemented in high-level browser modules (src/browser/Page.zig, src/browser/Browser.zig).
recommendations: When editing element implementations or event-dispatch code, run the element HTML test suite and any related form/input click tests to detect regressions in attribute reflection, default initialization, and event ordering.
Avoid changing attribute↔property reflection rules, default values, or disabled/open/selected initialization without updating these fixtures; many tests assert initial states parsed from HTML.
Treat click activation and preventDefault semantics as fragile: preserve the current event ordering, activation timing, and state-visibility semantics unless updating all dependent tests.

If modifying form association, requestSubmit, or submitter selection logic, run end-to-end tests that exercise actual submission flows (unit tests plus network-invoking form tests) because behavior propagates to submission/network code.

Be aware that parser-produced initial states matter: changes in parsing (html5ever bridge) or element construction order can change test outcomes; co-change testing across parser, DOM, and element initialization is required.

src/browser/webapi/element/html.description: This folder contains Zig implementations of HTML element WebAPI bindings and their V8/JS bridge metadata. It maps element attributes and behaviors into the browser runtime (attribute getters/setters, lifecycle hooks, JS constructors/prototypes, conversions to core Element/Node) and integrates those behaviors with Page-level services (URL resolution, form submission, mutation APIs, custom element registry, and drawing/context factories). These bindings are the boundary between the DOM core and the JS environment and are relied on by navigation, script execution and CDP interactions.
key_behaviors: Element creation and JS registration: constructors, JsApi metadata and prototype wiring so elements are exposed to V8.
Attribute/property mapping: getters/setters translate JS-accessible properties to underlying Element attributes and interact with Page mutation APIs.
Lifecycle and upgrade flows: custom element upgrade, connected/disconnected/attributeChanged callbacks, and build-time hooks (e.g., parsing inline onload).

Cross-module integrations: resolving navigation-related URLs (anchors, forms) against the Page base/URL module, creating/managing drawing contexts for canvas elements via a page factory, and invoking form submission via Page.submitForm.
Type conversion and identity: asElement/asNode conversions for DOM interoperability and caching/identity semantics (notably canvas getContext caching and form owner resolution).

insights.title: Anchor rel property missing from element facade
insights.problem: HTMLAnchorElement provided relList but lacked a rel string accessor, causing inconsistencies with scripts expecting a rel property.
insights.root_cause: The wrapper exposed the token list helper but did not expose the corresponding serialized string attribute.
insights.solution: Ensure both token-list and serialized-string accessors are provided for attributes that have dual representations (relList + rel) so JS code that reads/writes the serialized attribute behaves consistently.

insights.title: Canvas getContext returned multiple incompatible objects
insights.problem: getContext("2d") created fresh CanvasRenderingContext2D instances on each call and permitted mixing context types, breaking identity assumptions and wasting memory.
insights.root_cause: No cached context object or type locking; created contexts lacked a back-reference to the parent canvas.
insights.solution: Cache created contexts keyed by context type, lock the canvas to a single context type once created, and ensure created contexts hold a reference back to their Canvas element to support identity checks and lifecycle management.

insights.title: Body onload aliasing to window.onload introduces implicit cross-object linkage
insights.problem: body.onload was special-cased to alias to window.onload and inline onload attributes were parsed/installed at build time, so changes to either object must keep the alias semantics intact.
insights.root_cause: The body element and the global window both expose an onload slot; HTML historically aliases them causing tight coupling.
insights.solution: Implement the aliasing explicitly at element creation/parse-time and ensure mutation APIs and event handler installation routes properly update the window property when body.onload changes (and vice versa) to keep semantics deterministic.

insights.title: Form owner resolution and submission must respect attribute vs ancestry
insights.problem: Buttons and submitters need to resolve their associated form either via a form attribute or by ancestor traversal; submission logic must validate submitter and resolve action URLs via the Page.
insights.root_cause: Form-associated elements have two distinct association mechanisms and submission involves cross-module behavior (URL resolution, validation, and Page.submitForm).
insights.solution: Consistently implement form-owner resolution that checks the form attribute first, then ancestors; use Page helpers to resolve actions to absolute URLs and centralize submission validation to prevent duplication and mismatches.

insights.title: Custom element upgrade and callback invocation require precise flagging and scope control
insights.problem: Invoking connected/attributeChanged callbacks and running user constructors must respect per-element invocation flags and JS execution scopes; incorrect ordering or double-invocation will break web compatibility.
insights.root_cause: Custom element lifecycle crosses Zig runtime and V8 execution boundaries and depends on correct bookkeeping of upgrade state and callback re-entrancy flags.
insights.solution: Track upgrade/connected/attributeChanged invocation flags per element; run user-provided constructors and callbacks in the correct JS-local scope and ensure upgrade flows (including customized built-ins via 'is') attach definitions before invoking callbacks.

key_files.why: Implements anchor URL manipulation and resolution against the page base; touches URL semantics relied on by navigation and scripts.
Manages canvas context creation/caching and OffscreenCanvas transfer, affecting rendering/identity and memory behavior.
Handles form control iteration, action resolution and submission flows that call into Page.submitForm and validate submitters.
Coordinates custom element upgrades and lifecycle callback invocation across the Zig/V8 boundary, a sensitive cross-cutting concern.
Implements special-case body.onload aliasing to window.onload and build-time inline onload parsing; affects global event handler semantics.

construct_relationships: Every element wrapper exposes asElement/asNode conversions so JS-facing types map to core DOM Element/Node used by the parser and Page runtime.
JsApi metadata in each file registers constructors/prototypes with the V8 bridge; these registrations are the pathway for JS to construct and interact with underlying Zig element objects.
Anchor getters/setters delegate URL parsing and serialization to the project's URL module and resolve relative URLs against the Page base URL.
Canvas.getContext/transferControlToOffscreen interacts with a Page-level drawing context factory to create CanvasRenderingContext2D/WebGL and caches the context per canvas instance.

Form element methods (getElements, submit, requestSubmit) use Page APIs (resolve action, submitForm) to perform network submission and rely on element->form owner resolution logic implemented in button/control wrappers.
Custom element upgrade code interacts with a shared CustomElementRegistry and must invoke user constructors and lifecycle callbacks using V8 scopes to ensure correct semantics.
notable_relationships: These HTML element bindings depend on higher-level runtime modules: Page (for mutation APIs, action/URL resolution, and submission), the URL module (for href/action resolution), and the V8 snapshot/ScriptManager (for running custom element constructors and inline onload handlers).
Many wrappers are thin adapters; changing attribute/prop behavior requires ensuring compatibility with the DOM core model and Page mutation tracking so events and CDP-visible state remain consistent.

HTML runner tests referenced in some files indicate element behavior is verified by integration tests that exercise parsing, attribute mutation and script interactions—modifications must preserve test expectations.
recommendations: Preserve asElement/asNode conversion invariants and JsApi prototype/constructor registrations when editing wrappers; these are the primary JS<->DOM boundary contracts.
When changing attribute/property behavior, ensure mutations go through the Page mutation API so eventing, serialization and CDP-visible state remain coherent.

Respect identity and caching rules (e.g., canvas contexts): avoid returning new objects on repeated accessor calls unless spec semantics allow it, and maintain parent/back-reference links where callers rely on object identity.
Custom element lifecycle code must maintain per-element callback flags and use the same JS execution scope semantics as current implementations to avoid double-invocation or ordering regressions.

Any changes that touch URL resolution (anchors, forms, base elements) must use the Page/base URL and the URL module consistently; run the HTML runner/unit tests that exercise navigation/form behaviors after edits.

src/browser/tests/page/modules.description: A set of JavaScript ES module fixtures used by the browser page tests to exercise the runtime module loader and import semantics. The files encode scenarios for static and dynamic circular imports, dynamic import chains, re-exports, shared module state, self-importing async modules, and deliberate failures (syntax error / 404) so the test harness can validate resolution, caching, partially-initialized bindings, promise resolution, and error propagation.
key_behaviors: Static circular import resolution and partially-initialized binding observation across two-way imports
Dynamic import chains and promise-based resolution ordering across multiple dynamically-imported modules
Mixed static+dynamic circular dependency patterns (static entry points that dynamically import sides) and self-importing async modules
Module re-exporting and import forwarding to verify name resolution and aliasing behavior
Failure modes: module syntax errors and network/404 import failures to validate loader error propagation and test assertions
Shared module instance/caching behavior via module-scoped state used by multiple importers

insights.title: Static circular imports must present partially-initialized bindings consistently
insights.problem: Tests rely on observing values from a module that is still initializing due to a circular static import; incorrect loader ordering or eager copying would hide this behavior.
insights.root_cause: Circular import semantics in ES modules expose live bindings and allow access to values before full initialization; an importer that resolves values eagerly or clones initial values will diverge from spec.
insights.solution: Loader must provide live bindings and preserve initialization order semantics so tests in circular-a.js and circular-b.js reliably observe partially initialized states.

insights.title: Dynamic import chains must propagate promise resolution and preserve module caching
insights.problem: Dynamic-chain modules expect that dynamically imported modules resolve in sequence and that repeated imports reuse the same module instance.
insights.root_cause: Dynamic imports are asynchronous and return Promises; incorrect promise sequencing or creating new module instances per import can break tests.
insights.solution: Ensure dynamic import returns a Promise that resolves when the target module is evaluated, that evaluation follows transitive imports (a -> b -> c), and that module instances are cached after first evaluation.

insights.title: Mixing static and dynamic circular dependencies introduces ordering sensitivity
insights.problem: Tests which mix static and dynamic circular imports (mixed-circular-*) assume a specific evaluation ordering and that dynamic import can observe static-exported values or vice versa.
insights.root_cause: Interleaving synchronous static resolution with asynchronous dynamic import can create races if loader implementation does not respect spec ordering or binding liveness.
insights.solution: The loader must coordinate static evaluation steps with dynamic import evaluation so test expectations about visible values and timing are preserved.

insights.title: Syntax and network import failures must surface as import rejections
insights.problem: Files like syntax-error.js, test-syntax-error.js and test-404.js are used to assert that the importer surfaces parse errors and network 404s correctly; failing to propagate these leads to silent failures or different error types.
insights.root_cause: Differences in where/when parse and network errors are detected (fetch layer vs module parser vs host runtime) can change observable behavior.
insights.solution: Error pathways must reject the dynamic import Promise or cause module import failure in the same manner the tests expect, preserving error messages/types as much as possible.

insights.title: Module-scoped state must be shared across importers
insights.problem: shared.js increments and exposes a counter; if modules are evaluated multiple times or not cached, tests will observe diverging counters.
insights.root_cause: Module caching or instantiation mistakes (re-evaluating module source per import) cause multiple independent stateful instances.
insights.solution: Module instances must be cached keyed by resolved specifier so shared state is preserved across importers in the test harness.

key_files.why: One half of the static circular import pair; critical to tests that validate partially-initialized bindings and import ordering.
The counterpart to circular-a.js; used together to assert correct circular resolution semantics.
Entry point for the dynamic import chain tests (a -> b -> c); represents promise sequencing and caching behavior for dynamic imports.
Exercises re-exporting and import forwarding from other fixtures (base, importer), important for name resolution and aliasing tests.
Deliberate parse-error fixture; used by tests that assert error reporting and import rejection behavior.

construct_relationships: circular-a.js imports circular-b.js (uses getFromB, aValue) while circular-b.js imports circular-a.js (uses getFromA, bValue) to form a static circular dependency that exercises partially-initialized live bindings.

dynamic-chain-a.js dynamically imports chain-b via loadChain; chain-b (dynamic-chain-b.js) dynamically imports chain-c via loadNext; chain-c (dynamic-chain-c.js) exports the terminal finalValue — together they form a promise-resolved import chain.
dynamic-circular-x.js and dynamic-circular-y.js implement a pair where X dynamically loads Y and Y dynamically loads X to verify circularity when dynamic import is involved.
mixed-circular-static.js provides static exports and exposes loadDynamicSide which dynamic modules (mixed-circular-dynamic.js) call to create mixed static/dynamic circular graphs.
re-exporter.js re-exports base.js and importer.js exports (baseValue, importedValue, localValue) to test that re-exported names refer to the original live bindings.
shared.js provides increment/getCount that multiple importer modules invoke to assert module caching and shared instance semantics.
self_async.js awaits import of itself to validate self-import and that dynamic import of the same specifier resolves to the already-evaluating module or appropriate Promise behavior.
notable_relationships: These fixture modules are consumed by the page-level test harness and therefore are tightly coupled to the runtime module loader implementation in the browser: changes here affect tests that exercise ScriptManager/V8 integration and the module fetching layer.

Import failure fixtures (test-404.js) interact with the network loader layer (network/http.zig) so test expectations assume that module specifier fetches can produce HTTP 404 and that such errors propagate back into the module loader and test assertions.

Syntax-error fixtures exercise the parser path used by the JS runtime (V8) and any pre-parsing or static analysis performed by the module loader; differences in where parsing happens will change observed error timing and messages.

Dynamic import behaviors in these files depend on Promise semantics and the environment's event loop scheduling — the test harness relies on deterministic ordering that the ScriptManager/V8 integration must maintain.
recommendations: When editing or renaming any module files, preserve their file paths and exported symbol names or update all tests that import them; tests reference these exact specifiers.

Do not change the observable timing/ordering semantics of circular or dynamic imports without adjusting tests; these fixtures are specifically designed to assert subtle ordering and partially-initialized binding behaviors.
Keep modules that intentionally error (syntax-error.js, test-404.js) as failing fixtures; if their contents are modified, update test expectations accordingly.

Any change that affects module caching, evaluation count, or binding mutability should be validated by running the page-level module tests (unit/integration) and end-to-end test suites that exercise the module loader and V8 integration.

Because these fixtures validate interactions with the network loader and V8, run the relevant integration tests after edits (module loader tests, ScriptManager/V8 snapshot flows, and any CDP-driven page tests) to catch regressions in error propagation or promise resolution ordering.

src/browser/webapi/event.description: This folder implements DOM event types and their JS bindings used by pages: concrete event structs (KeyboardEvent, MouseEvent, MessageEvent, etc.), a shared UIEvent base, constructors for trusted/untrusted creation, and the JsApi bridges that expose these types to the embedded JS runtime. It matters because event construction, property semantics, V8 handle ownership and prototype wiring are central to correct DOM behavior, input handling, messaging, and safe lifetime management when events cross the Zig/V8 boundary.
key_behaviors: Creation and initialization of DOM event instances (trusted vs untrusted), including legacy init* methods where required.
Binding event types into the JS runtime via JsApi bridges and populating event prototypes so scripts can construct/inspect events.
Event subtype composition and casting (UIEvent -> MouseEvent -> PointerEvent/WheelEvent) to present correct properties for input flows.
Management of payloads and JS-owned values (strings, objects, Blobs, Promise handles) with explicit ownership, deinit/release semantics and discriminated unions for multiple data representations.
Exposure of event properties and constants (e.g., deltaMode, pointerType, modifier state) and parsing/normalization of input options for use by scripts and dispatching infrastructure.

insights.title: Explicit release of JS-owned values is required
insights.problem: Several event types hold JS values (error objects, message payloads, promise handles) and failing to release them leads to leaks or dangling references across the Zig/V8 boundary.
insights.root_cause: Events manage V8/js.Value handles manually and Zig has no GC for these cross-language references; lifecycle is tied to explicit init/deinit and event dispatch timing.
insights.solution: Every event type that stores a JS handle must implement deterministic deinit/release and preserve that contract when changing struct layout or ownership semantics; edits must keep the matching ref/release pairing and consider discriminated unions' release paths.

insights.title: MessageEvent.data supports multiple binary representations
insights.problem: PostMessage/WebSocket binaryType 'blob' and other binary forms require representing data as different kinds (string, arraybuffer, Blob pointer) and correct release semantics.
insights.root_cause: Original single js.Value.Temp storage was insufficient for blob pointer ownership and binary variants.
insights.solution: MessageEvent uses a discriminated union for data variants; any changes must preserve the union shape and the rules for converting/from JS and releasing the active variant.

insights.title: Trusted vs untrusted construction and legacy initializers are used throughout
insights.problem: Code relies on distinguishing trusted events (synthesized by browsing context/system) from untrusted ones (script-created) and some web APIs expect legacy init methods (initCustomEvent, initTextEvent).
insights.root_cause: Web platform semantics require provenance and compatibility with older APIs; consumers in other modules assume those semantics.
insights.solution: Retain and respect trusted/untrusted constructors and legacy init* methods; any signature or behavior change must be reflected in JsApi bridge registration and consumers that create events programmatically.

insights.title: Input event hierarchy and subtype composition must stay consistent
insights.problem: MouseEvent, PointerEvent, WheelEvent, KeyboardEvent and InputEvent share fields and are cast/treated polymorphically by dispatchers and script-exposed prototypes.
insights.root_cause: Event dispatch and handlers expect predictable subtype relationships (e.g., PointerEvent composed from MouseEvent fields). Breaking composition or casting helpers will alter dispatch behavior and script-visible properties.
insights.solution: When modifying fields or constructors, maintain the composition/casting helpers and constants (deltaMode, pointerType, button mappings) so existing code treating events polymorphically continues to work.

insights.title: Event types are registered into the JS bridge and page factory
insights.problem: Event constructors and prototypes are wired into the page factory / JsApi; renaming or changing bindings without updating registration will break script-side construction and tests.
insights.root_cause: JsApi registration is explicit per-type and page allocation uses a shared arena/factory model.
insights.solution: Any API/name/constructor changes must be coordinated with JsApi registration code and the page arena/factory allocation patterns; keep tests up-to-date that validate constructor exposure and prototype flags.

key_files.why: Defines the shared UIEvent base, option parsing and subtype casting helpers used by most specialized input events.
Manages complex data payload representation and lifetime (discriminated union for blob/arraybuffer/string) — a high-risk file for memory/ownership correctness.
Implements mouse/pointer base semantics, coordinates, buttons and subtype propagation used by PointerEvent and WheelEvent.
Composes pointer-specific properties on top of MouseEvent; changes affect detailed pointer metadata exposed to scripts.
Introduces first-class formdata event semantics and binds FormData payloads into the JS bridge — relevant where form submit flows or FormData dispatch occur.

construct_relationships: UIEvent is the common base used by FocusEvent, KeyboardEvent, InputEvent, MouseEvent and TextEvent; these types rely on UIEvent init and accessor helpers.
MouseEvent provides fields and helpers that PointerEvent composes/extends; WheelEvent is a specialized MouseEvent with delta fields and constants.
Many event types expose JsApi bridges that register constructors/prototypes with the embedded JS runtime; those bridges depend on the page factory/arena for allocation.
MessageEvent and PromiseRejectionEvent store JS-owned values and must coordinate with V8 handle lifetimes managed elsewhere (the JsApi/ScriptManager layer).
SubmitEvent and FormDataEvent are expected by form handling flows in higher-level page/navigation code and must carry submitter/FormData pointers for dispatch consumers.
notable_relationships: JsApi bindings in these files are coupled to the V8 integration (ScriptManager / JS bridge module); changes to event JsApi signatures require matching updates where JsApi is consumed/registered.

Event allocation and lifetime use the page arena/factory patterns and interact with Page/Browser dispatching logic (src/browser/Page.zig, Browser/EventManager) — event objects are created for dispatch and must remain valid for the dispatch lifecycle.

MessageEvent/Blob handling intersects with other subsystems that represent Blobs or ArrayBuffers (resource/memory management code) and with network layers that produce binary payloads (src/network/http.zig producing responses).

Trusted/untrusted semantics and legacy init methods affect consumers across the codebase (script-executed events vs internal node-generated events) and may influence security-sensitive behavior in higher-level modules.

Unit tests included alongside many event files indicate expected runtime behavior; some end-to-end tests rely on these events reaching JS consumers via the CDP/server path (src/mcp/* and CDP domains).
recommendations: Preserve explicit deinit/release logic for any event that holds JS handles or native pointers; ensure all code paths (including early returns) free owned resources.
Do not change the MessageEvent data discriminant layout or lifetime semantics without updating conversion/release code and dependent consumers that inspect the variant.
Maintain trusted/untrusted constructor contracts and legacy init* method behavior; update JsApi registration in tandem with any signature/name changes so constructors remain available to scripts.
When modifying fields or constructors of UIEvent or MouseEvent, check all subtype composition and casting helpers (PointerEvent, WheelEvent, FocusEvent, etc.) to avoid breaking polymorphic dispatch.

Run the folder’s unit tests and higher-level event dispatch tests after edits; also exercise end-to-end flows that exercise postMessage, form submit, input/mouse events and promise rejection handling to detect integration regressions.

src/browser/webapi/net.description: This folder implements the browser-facing networking WebAPIs exposed to page JavaScript: Fetch, XMLHttpRequest, Headers, Request, Response, FormData, URLSearchParams, WebSocket and the XHR event-target mixin. It sits between the page runtime/DOM and the native HttpClient/connection layer and therefore coordinates promise lifecycle, event dispatch, body/header conversion, timeout/abort semantics and resource ownership for network activity.
key_behaviors: Translate JS API calls (fetch(), new XMLHttpRequest(), new WebSocket(), FormData/URLSearchParams usage) into HttpClient network operations and yield JS-visible Responses/Events/Promises.
Manage request/response header normalization and serialization and convert incremental network callbacks into JS-facing buffered bodies, streams or events.

Coordinate lifecycle concerns: AbortSignal cancellation, XHR readyState transitions and event dispatch (progress/load/error/timeout/abort), timeouts enforced at the native client, and reference counting to ensure requests don't free while callbacks remain outstanding.
Bridge between webapi objects (Request/Response/Headers/FormData/URLSearchParams) and underlying HttpClient responses, including special-casing local blob: URLs and producing synthetic Responses.
Provide WebSocket framing/event dispatch and ensure WebSocket connections are resource-isolated from the HTTP connection pool to avoid cross-resource starvation.

insights.title: SameSite cookie origin propagation required
insights.problem: Fetch requests (and other fetch-like flows) did not pass the page's URL as cookie_origin, breaking SameSite cookie behavior for fetch/XHR.
insights.root_cause: The cookie origin parameter was not threaded from the page/request context into the HttpClient request headers/cookie handling layer.
insights.solution: Always propagate the page's URL (or appropriate request origin) as the cookie_origin when creating network requests so HttpClient can apply SameSite and origin-aware cookie rules.

insights.title: HttpClient.Response vs earlier Transfer abstraction
insights.problem: WebAPI Response and XHR plumbing referenced an older Transfer type; callbacks and ownership semantics became mismatched after HttpClient was refactored to return HttpClient.Response.
insights.root_cause: A refactor changed HttpClient callback signatures and the response container type but webapi modules were not fully updated to use the new Response/iterator API and ownership/refcounting conventions.
insights.solution: Treat webapi Response objects as potentially owning or referencing an HttpClient.Response: use the updated HttpClient callbacks and header iterator API, ensure Response tracks and can abort/terminate underlying HttpClient.Response, and update refcounting to reflect new lifecycle.

insights.title: Header iteration and populate signature divergence
insights.problem: Header population and iteration were inconsistent: some modules referenced net_http.Headers or used Transfer-era header handling, while newer HttpClient exposes a headerIterator and a renamed http import.
insights.root_cause: API renames and signature simplifications in the network layer were not propagated consistently across Headers, Request, Response and XHR code.
insights.solution: Adopt the single canonical http import and header iteration API across all webapi networking modules so header normalization, serialization and iteration behave consistently and callers don't need to pass extraneous arenas or arguments.

insights.title: Active request reference counting and callback ordering
insights.problem: XMLHttpRequest acquisition/release semantics could leak or double-free active request references if scheduling failed or callbacks outlived the originating JS scope; similar concerns exist for Fetch promise resolution and Response cloning.
insights.root_cause: Asynchronous native callbacks (HttpClient) and synchronous JS APIs need careful refcounting; changes to when refs were acquired (after scheduling) exposed edge cases where failures left refs unbalanced.
insights.solution: Ensure every scheduling/acquire step has a corresponding failure path that releases refs; keep clear ownership rules that native callbacks hold a reference for the duration of network activity and webapi objects release or clone the underlying native response consistently.

insights.title: FormData and XHR event behavior must match DOM expectations
insights.problem: Constructing FormData(form) did not dispatch the 'formdata' event; XHR lacked end-to-end timeout support and did not emit timeout events.
insights.root_cause: Event dispatch hooks were missing from FormData construction paths and XHR did not wire its timeout property through to native curl timeouts and event dispatch.
insights.solution: When constructing FormData from a form element, dispatch a 'formdata' event on that form. For XHR, propagate the timeout property to the native client and ensure timeout events are emitted and handlers invoked consistently with readyState changes.

insights.title: WebSocket connections must not starve HTTP connection pool
insights.problem: WebSocket implementation reused the generic HTTP connection pool, allowing pages to exhaust pool capacity with WebSocket handshakes or long-lived sockets and starve HTTP requests.
insights.root_cause: WebSockets were implemented atop the same connection pool configured for HTTP requests without any per-protocol isolation or limits.
insights.solution: Give WebSocket connections a distinct pool and enforce concurrency limits so WebSocket activity cannot block ordinary HTTP requests; consider per-page or global caps consistent with existing connection management semantics.

key_files.why: Primary bridge for fetch() — composes Request, triggers HttpClient, and resolves JS promises; central for request lifecycle, blob: handling and AbortSignal interaction.
Implements XHR lifecycle, readyState transitions, progress/timeout/abort dispatch and native callback wiring — changes here affect many testable browser behaviors.
Represents HTTP responses exposed to JS, converts bodies to promised types, and must coordinate ownership with HttpClient.Response; impacts fetch/XHR stream behavior.
Canonical header normalization/serialization and JS-visible Headers API; used when building requests and when exposing response headers.
Implements WebSocket API, framing and events and has resource management implications (separate connection pool and concurrency limits).

construct_relationships: Fetch (Fetch.zig) composes Request and Headers, registers HttpClient callbacks, and produces Response objects which may reference an HttpClient.Response; it also consults AbortSignal and resolves/rejects JS Promises via the Page runtime.

XMLHttpRequest (XMLHttpRequest.zig) owns per-request state, uses XMLHttpRequestEventTarget for event handlers, drives HttpClient requests, buffers incremental data into Response-style conversions, and dispatches DOM progress/readyState events back through the Page event manager.

Response (Response.zig) provides body conversion helpers (text/json/arrayBuffer/blob/bytes) and cloning semantics consumed by Fetch and XHR; it must manage an underlying HttpClient.Response pointer and support aborting/terminating that native response.

Headers (Headers.zig) is the canonical header store and exposes populateHttpHeader functionality used when serializing requests for HttpClient; Request/Response rely on it for normalization and iteration.

FormData (FormData.zig) is a producer of URL-encoded serialized bodies and integrates with URLSearchParams for encoding; constructing FormData from a form must trigger a form-level 'formdata' event in the DOM.

WebSocket (WebSocket.zig) integrates with the lower-level connection management but must be isolated from the HTTP connection pool; it dispatches open/message/close/error events through the page event system similar to XHR.
notable_relationships: This subtree depends on the network layer in src/network/http.zig (HttpClient and HttpClient.Response signatures) — changes to HttpClient callback signatures, header iteration, or connection pool semantics must be reflected here.

Event dispatch and JS promise resolution flow into Page/ScriptManager/V8 bindings (page runtime) — webapi code must obey the page arena allocation lifetimes, js.Function.Temp usage patterns and the Page event manager invocation semantics.
Cookie handling and SameSite behavior are handled elsewhere (cookie store/cookie manager) and expect cookie_origin to be supplied by network requests originating from this folder.
Blob handling and synthetic blob: Responses rely on in-memory blob storage and DOM Blob semantics provided by other browser modules; the webapi network code must respect those blob lifetimes.

Global build and ABI considerations: naming/import changes (net_http -> http, http.Method) reflect cross-module refactors — edits here must be consistent with import paths and type names used in other modules.
recommendations: Preserve AbortSignal checks and ensure cancellation is honored during both header and body callbacks to avoid delivering stale data or leaking native resources.

Maintain strict acquire/release semantics for active requests: any path that acquires a ref must have a matching release on scheduling failure and on completion/error; validate refcount behavior with tests.
When modifying callback signatures or header iteration, update all callers in this folder to use the unified http import and header iterator pattern; check for lingering net_http references.
Ensure the page URL or appropriate request origin is threaded into network request creation as cookie_origin so SameSite cookie semantics and cookie tests remain correct.
If altering WebSocket connection behavior, respect the requirement for an isolated WebSocket connection pool and enforce concurrency limits to avoid starving HTTP requests.

Respect JS binding lifetimes: use the Page arena for short-lived allocations and js.Function.Temp for persisted callbacks; ensure promise resolution and event dispatch occur on the expected page/main-thread context.

Run the project's unit tests and higher-level e2e tests after edits (make test; full end-to-end demo/e2e where relevant) because subtle regressions in headers, cookies, timeouts, or event dispatch can break client-visible behavior.

src/browser/webapi/collections.description: This subtree implements live DOM collection types and JS-visible collection/iterator adapters used by pages (NodeList, HTMLCollection, RadioNodeList, DOMTokenList, etc.). It centralizes traversal and caching (TreeWalker/NodeLive/ChildNodes) and exposes stable JS semantics (indexed access, length, forEach, iteration) while managing lifetime/allocator interactions with the page arena and JS bridge.
key_behaviors: Provide live, incremental indexed access to node sequences (children, document-wide, filtered sets) with version-aware caching to avoid full re-traversals on every access.

Wrap internal Zig collection/traversal state into JS-visible objects (NodeList/HTMLCollection/RadioNodeList/DOMTokenList) via the page factory/bridge and expose JS iterator semantics (next/values/keys/entries/forEach).
Map DOM attribute-backed collections (DOMTokenList) and element-specific collections (HTMLOptionsCollection, HTMLFormControlsCollection) to live views that reflect underlying attribute/state changes.
Generate JS iterator objects from arbitrary internal iterators (iterator.Entry) translating Zig semantics (optionals/errors) into JS next() shapes while managing reference lifetimes.
Handle special-case semantics required by web platform APIs (callable HTMLAllCollection behavior, namedItem radio-group -> RadioNodeList conversion, selectedIndex forwarding for select/options).

insights.title: Allocator/Arena mismatch when creating JS wrappers
insights.problem: NodeList/other JS-visible wrappers were sometimes created with a different allocator (page factory) than the NodeLive/ChildNodes arena that owns the backing data, causing mismatched lifetimes and potential use-after-free or allocator errors.
insights.root_cause: Factory-based wrapper creation used a different allocator/context than the collection backing store (page arena), so wrapper lifetime didn’t align with node backing lifetimes.
insights.solution: Ensure JS-visible wrappers are allocated from the same page arena or otherwise tied to the backing store’s lifetime (create wrappers within the backing arena or adopt a consistent allocator strategy).

insights.title: Iterator wrappers did not consistently manage reference counts
insights.problem: forEach/values created iterators that referenced NodeList or collection state without explicit acquire/release pairing, risking dangling references or retained resources.
insights.root_cause: The Entry iterator generator previously inspected inner types without standardized RC semantics; iterator objects lacked explicit RC fields and lifecycle operations.
insights.solution: Iterator wrappers must store explicit RC/reference handles to the underlying collection and enforce acquire/release semantics on creation and disposal so JS iteration does not outlive the backing collection.

insights.title: Versioned caching for length/index can become stale across DOM mutations
insights.problem: Cached length/last-index optimizations improve sequential access but can return incorrect results if DOM mutations and version bumps are not detected or if traversal assumptions are violated.
insights.root_cause: Optimistic caching keyed by a DOM version counter relies on accurate version bumps on all mutation paths and correct invalidation of cached indices.
insights.solution: All mutation paths must increment the DOM version; collection getters must compare stored version and re-walk/truncate caches when version mismatches are detected to preserve live semantics.

insights.title: Named-item semantics require special-case RadioNodeList creation
insights.problem: Form control namedItem must return a RadioNodeList when multiple radio inputs share a name; treating it as a simple collection lookup breaks expected web platform behavior.
insights.root_cause: Generic name/id lookup returns a single element or collection but omits the radio-group-to-RadioNodeList mapping mandated by HTML forms API.
insights.solution: Named-item resolution must detect multiple matching radio controls and construct a RadioNodeList view that implements value semantics and indexed access consistent with platform expectations.

insights.title: DOMTokenList must reflect live attribute changes
insights.problem: Token list operations (add/remove/toggle/replace) must remain in sync with the underlying attribute string and expose live reads to JS; decoupling the token list from the attribute causes inconsistencies.
insights.root_cause: DOMTokenList is a live view; implementations that cache tokens without robust write-through/read-back to the attribute risk divergence.
insights.solution: Implement DOMTokenList operations as live operations that read/modify the backing attribute and ensure iteration/contains/length reflect the current attribute on every access or on appropriate invalidation.

key_files.why: Parametric TreeWalker-based generator that underpins most live collection types (HTMLCollection variants, HTMLAllCollection) and implements caching/versioning strategies.
Central JS-visible NodeList wrapper with multiple backing strategies and iterator/forEach integration; a frequent bridge between internal collections and JS.
Specialized live childNodes implementation optimized for incremental access and a known source of allocator/ar​ena lifetime concerns when creating JS wrappers.
Generic iterator Entry generator that adapts internal iterators to JS next() semantics and is responsible for RC/lifecycle handling of iteration objects.
Hosts form controls namedItem behavior and radio-group -> RadioNodeList conversion; encodes special-case platform semantics relied on by forms APIs.

construct_relationships: NodeLive (node_live.zig) produces traversal state and getAtIndex/getByName primitives used by HTMLCollection, HTMLAllCollection, HTMLOptionsCollection, and HTMLFormControlsCollection.
ChildNodes implements a child-specific NodeLive-like backing and provides cached length/last-index that NodeList runtime wrappers consume for JS-visible childNodes access.

NodeList wraps various backing sources (ChildNodes, selector results, RadioNodeList, named lists) and exposes JS iteration methods; NodeList.forEach and iterator creation rely on iterator.Entry to produce JS-consumable iterators.

iterator.Entry (iterator.zig) adapts internal Zig iterator return shapes to JS next() objects and must hold explicit RC references to the underlying NodeList/collection that produced the inner iterator.

HTMLFormControlsCollection delegates iteration/length to an underlying HTMLCollection and, when namedItem yields multiple radio inputs, constructs a RadioNodeList view implemented as a NodeList-like wrapper that maps value get/set to radio.checked state.

DOMTokenList manipulates an element attribute directly and must reflect attribute changes immediately; it is conceptually backed by the element and interacts with the same arena/lifetime discipline as other collection wrappers.
notable_relationships: These collections are tightly coupled to the page arena/factory and the JS bridge surface: allocator mismatches between factory-created JS wrappers and collection backings can lead to lifetime errors visible across the Zig/V8 boundary.

Caching/versioning relies on global DOM version increments which are affected by any DOM mutation code elsewhere in the runtime (parsing, script-driven mutations, tree reparenting). Mutations outside this folder must bump version counters consistently.

Iterator lifecycle and RC semantics interact with the garbage/lifetime model exposed to V8 via ScriptManager; iterator objects escaping to JS must hold resources valid for the JS-visible lifetime and be released when iteration completes.

Special-case API parity (callable HTMLAllCollection, RadioNodeList value semantics, HTMLOptionsCollection selectedIndex interactions) are expected by external clients (Playwright/Puppeteer/CDP) and serialization; changes here affect compatibility with consumers.
DOMTokenList and attribute-backed behavior create a tight coupling with element attribute storage; cross-file changes to attribute representation or element arenas will impact token list correctness.
recommendations: Preserve allocator/arena alignment: when producing JS-visible wrappers, allocate from or tie the wrapper to the same page arena/backing store used by the underlying nodes to avoid lifetime/allocator mismatches.

Maintain explicit acquire/release/refcounting for any iterator or JS object that references internal collections; ensure iterator.Entry includes consistent RC semantics and that forEach/values create and dispose references safely.
When modifying caching/versioning logic, verify that all DOM mutation sites increment the DOM version and add tests for mutation scenarios that exercise cached length/last-index invalidation paths.

Respect web-platform special-case semantics: namedItem -> RadioNodeList conversion, HTMLAllCollection callable behavior, and HTMLOptionsCollection selectedIndex forwarding must remain consistent with existing API contracts.

Run the repository format check (zig build fmt) and the unit/integration tests (make test); for changes affecting lifetimes or JS bridging, run end-to-end scenarios that exercise iteration, forEach, and cross-language wrapper lifetimes (CDP-driven clients) to catch allocator/lifetime regressions.

src/browser/webapi/streams.description: This folder implements the Web Streams API primitives used by pages and internal wiring: ReadableStream, WritableStream, TransformStream, their controllers, and default reader/writer types. It bridges Zig runtime constructs with page-local JS/V8 callbacks and promise machinery to provide enqueue/pull/write/close/error flows, piping, async iteration and backpressure management that the rest of the browser runtime depends on.
key_behaviors: Stream lifecycle management: creation, start, enqueue, pull, close, error, and finalization of readable and writable streams.
Locking and exclusivity: readers lock readable streams and writers lock writable streams; releaseLock/cancel/close transitions update state and resolve/reject pending operations.
Backpressure and queuing: controllers compute desiredSize, schedule pull calls, and maintain internal queues for chunks and pending promises.
JS callback integration and reentrancy: underlying source/sink/transform functions are invoked via the JS bridge (V8), with results mapped to Zig promises and resolved/rejected accordingly.
Piping and transform flow: pipeTo/pipeThrough and TransformStream glue readable and writable lifecycles, propagate errors/closures, and coordinate flush/transform hooks.

insights.title: Race between controller actions and pending reads/writes
insights.problem: Enqueue/close/error calls can race with pending read() or write() promises leading to missed resolutions, double-fulfillment, or inconsistent state.
insights.root_cause: Asynchronous JS callbacks and Zig scheduling can mutate queues and stream state from multiple call sites without a single serialized control path.
insights.solution: Maintain strict ordering and atomic state transitions when updating queues and promise lists; always check current stream state before resolving/rejecting promises and ensure that only one code path resolves a given promise.

insights.title: Promises must be resolved/rejected in the correct JS turn and scope
insights.problem: Resolving/rejecting promises directly from Zig without using the page-local JS scope or correct microtask timing can cause V8 reentrancy bugs or lost callbacks.
insights.root_cause: Controller and reader/writer code executes in native Zig context but must cooperate with V8's promise/microtask model and the page-local JS execution scope.
insights.solution: Use the provided page-local JS scope facilities when creating/resolving promises and schedule promise resolution in the appropriate task/microtask context used elsewhere in the runtime.

insights.title: Desired size computation drives pull scheduling and memory usage
insights.problem: Incorrect desiredSize calculations or delayed pull scheduling can lead to either excessive memory accumulation in queues or underutilization of upstream producers.
insights.root_cause: Controllers must compute desiredSize consistently across different chunk types and respect byte vs chunk-based sizing semantics; pull scheduling must respect current queue state.
insights.solution: Ensure desiredSize logic matches the Streams spec semantics for the controller and that shouldCallPull/callPullIfNeeded are invoked atomically after queue/state updates.

insights.title: Errors must propagate across pipes and through readers/writers
insights.problem: When a stream in a pipe errors or when transform/flush callbacks throw, downstream/upstream streams and associated promises may not be consistently errored, leaving locks stuck or resources leaked.
insights.root_cause: Multiple moves between readable/writable/transform controllers plus JS exceptions create complex edge cases for propagating failure states.
insights.solution: Establish clear error propagation semantics: controllers should notify readers/writers, reject pending promises, and break pipe state in a single coordinated step; ensure transform/flush exceptions are caught and forwarded to the appropriate stream(s).

insights.title: Chunk type handling across JS/Zig boundary
insights.problem: Chunks can be typed arrays, strings, or arbitrary JS values; mishandling lifetimes or converting types incorrectly can corrupt data or leak memory.
insights.root_cause: Controllers and streams accept heterogeneous JS values but must map them into Zig-managed queue entries and ensure V8 roots and lifetimes are respected.
insights.solution: Use the established page-local JS rooting conventions when storing JS values in queues, and normalize chunk size/byteLength handling consistently for desiredSize/backpressure logic.

key_files.why: Core readable stream state machine: exposes pipeTo/pipeThrough, pull/cancel hooks, async iteration — central coordinator for readable-side semantics.
Manages the readable-side queue, resolves pending read promises, computes desiredSize and schedules pulls — key for backpressure and queuing behavior.
Implements writable-side state machine and sink integration; coordinates writer locks and forwards writes/close to underlying sinks or transforms.
Connects a writable and readable pair, invokes transform/flush functions and is the focal point for pipeThrough and transform semantics.
Provides the writer abstraction used by consumers to write/close and exposes ready/closed promises — essential for correct external usage of writable streams.

construct_relationships: ReadableStream holds or references a ReadableStreamDefaultController which manages the internal queue and triggers pull/cancel hooks.
ReadableStreamDefaultReader locks a ReadableStream and consults the controller to fulfill read() promises or queue pending reads.
WritableStream pairs with WritableStreamDefaultController; WritableStreamDefaultWriter acquires exclusive access to a WritableStream and forwards writes to the controller/underlying sink.
TransformStream composes an internal WritableStream and ReadableStream; the TransformStreamDefaultController mediates transform/flush hooks and enqueues transformed chunks into the readable side.

pipeTo/pipeThrough logic in ReadableStream orchestrates the creation of writers/readers and uses PipeState to coordinate backpressure, error forwarding, and closure across readable and writable streams.
notable_relationships: These stream primitives are tightly coupled to the JS bridge / V8 execution context: underlying source/sink/transform callbacks are invoked in page-local JS scopes and must obey V8 promise/microtask behavior.

Changes here affect any higher-level features that consume streams (navigation fetch/dump flows, network response bodies, or CDP-exposed streaming APIs) because they rely on correct stream semantics, backpressure, and lifetime management.
Memory and lifecycle concerns cross into the DOM and ScriptManager layers since queued JS values must be rooted and released according to page-local garbage collection expectations.
Piping and transform behavior can impact network throughput and resource usage elsewhere (e.g., network/http.zig consumers) due to backpressure propagation and pull scheduling.
recommendations: Preserve all stream state invariants and locking semantics: verify transitions (readable/writable states) are atomic and that only one code path resolves/rejects any pending promise.
When invoking or handling JS callbacks, use the page-local JS scope and ensure promise resolution/rejection happens in the correct microtask turn to avoid V8 reentrancy issues.
Maintain consistent desiredSize computation and ensure callPullIfNeeded/shouldCallPull are called immediately after queue/state changes to avoid backpressure regressions or memory growth.
Treat queued JS values and chunk conversions carefully: respect V8 rooting/lifetimes and existing conventions for typed arrays/strings to avoid use-after-free or memory leaks.

Run the project unit tests and any stream-related integration/e2e tests after edits (zig build test and higher-level E2E flows that exercise pipe/transform paths) and run the formatter/CI checks to ensure JS-binding and build expectations are preserved.

src/browser/webapi/element.description: This folder implements the WebAPI Element layer: concrete Element/Node wrappers, attribute storage and mapping, dataset conversions, SVG handling, and the JS/V8 bridge metadata that exposes HTML/SVG elements and their behaviors to script. It mediates between the DOM core (Element/Node, Page mutation APIs, CustomElementRegistry) and the JS environment, so changes here directly affect DOM semantics, JS-visible identity, and interactions with page-level services (URL resolution, drawing contexts, lifecycle callbacks).
key_behaviors: Attribute management and identity: attributes are stored, normalized, validated, and mapped into a per-page attribute lookup; mutations emit page notifications so the rest of the runtime (rendering, event dispatch, CDP) can react.

JS bridge exposure: each concrete HTML/SVG element registers JsApi metadata and constructors/prototypes so V8 can create and interact with element wrappers; property getters/setters translate JS properties into attribute operations.

Element lifecycle and upgrades: custom element upgrade, connected/disconnected/attributeChanged callback invocation and build-time hooks (e.g., parsing inline onload) are implemented and integrated with the CustomElementRegistry.

Property/attribute translation and helpers: DOMStringMap translates camelCase dataset properties to data- attributes (with SSO optimization), Anchor resolves/edits URL components against the page base, and many elements implement attribute-backed getters/setters.

Resource/host object management: elements that create or cache host objects (Canvas contexts, form-owner resolution, relList, etc.) manage lifetime, identity and reuse to meet spec constraints and avoid memory duplication.

insights.title: Attribute identity + page-level lookup is authoritative
insights.problem: Attributes must be identity-stable and mutations must be observable by the rest of the runtime; ad-hoc attribute allocations or missing notifications can break code that relies on object identity or change observation.
insights.root_cause: Attribute storage and NamedNodeMap are separate from element wrappers; without centralized lookup and consistent notifications, multiple Attr objects or silent mutations can appear.
insights.solution: Respect the per-Page attribute lookup (page._attribute_lookup) when creating/cloning attributes, always normalize/validate names via the provided helpers, and ensure all attribute mutations route through the mutation helpers that notify the Page.

insights.title: JsApi registrations are part of the runtime-visible API surface
insights.problem: Modifying element JsApi metadata (constructors/prototype shapes, property names) can desynchronize the V8 snapshot or break code that expects certain constructors and prototype names.
insights.root_cause: Element JsApi wiring is consumed by V8 at snapshot/initialization time and used by script expectations (tests, CDP clients, third-party scripts).
insights.solution: Any change to JsApi shapes or names must preserve public API expectations or trigger snapshot regeneration and full compatibility validation; preserve prototype registration names and constructors unless coordinated with snapshot/process that loads V8.

insights.title: Canvas context creation must preserve identity and lock context type
insights.problem: Returning fresh CanvasRenderingContext objects on each getContext call and allowing multiple incompatible context types lead to spec violations, identity confusion, and unnecessary allocation.
insights.root_cause: getContext previously created contexts without caching and without linking the context back to its parent Canvas element.
insights.solution: Cache and reuse created contexts per canvas and lock the canvas to a single context type; ensure created context objects hold a reference to their Canvas parent so operations that require the canvas can find it.

insights.title: dataset property conversions need to balance correctness and allocation
insights.problem: Frequent camelCase <-> kebab-case conversions can allocate and become hot paths, and long keys can incur extra allocations.
insights.root_cause: Dataset access exposes JS property names that must be mapped to attribute names; naive allocations for every access harm performance.
insights.solution: Use SSO for short keys and a clear fallback path for long names; maintain deterministic and reversible camel<->kebab conversion utilities and ensure they are used everywhere dataset mapping occurs.

insights.title: Some element behaviors are attribute-backed and also have special cross-object aliases
insights.problem: Attributes that map to global/window-level handlers or have associated auxiliary objects (e.g., body.onload -> window.onload, anchor.rel vs relList) require coordinated state so changes are visible in both places.
insights.root_cause: Attribute getters/setters are implemented on element wrappers but related behaviors live elsewhere (window, relList helper objects).
insights.solution: Keep attribute-backed getters/setters and the auxiliary mappings in sync: write through to attributes and update the related host objects or aliases during mutation notifications so both sides remain consistent.

key_files.why: Central attribute storage, name validation/normalization, identity mapping via page._attribute_lookup, and mutation notifications — core to any attribute-related change.

Orchestrates concrete HTML element types, innerHTML/outerHTML behaviors, attribute-to-event wiring, custom element construction and the Html.Type union — the central bridge between element implementations and lifecycle wiring.
Implements element.dataset behavior including camel<->kebab conversion and SSO optimizations — affects many attribute interactions and is a hot path for scripts.
Manages canvas context creation/caching and OffscreenCanvas transfer semantics — changes here alter object identity and rendering context lifecycle.
Implements URL resolution and URL-component getters/setters for anchors, coupling element attributes to the URL module and page base resolution behavior.

construct_relationships: Attribute/NamedNodeMap (Attribute.zig) stores attribute objects and exposes mutation helpers that call Page mutation APIs; element property getters/setters in Html.* files must use those helpers to keep Page notifications and identity consistent.
DOMStringMap maps camelCase JS property access to data- attributes on Element; it relies on Attribute/NamedNodeMap for underlying storage and must use normalize/validate helpers for name conversion.
Html.Type and concrete HTML/* Zig files register JsApi metadata and constructors; V8 snapshot/ScriptManager expects those registrations to exist and uses them to expose element types to JS.
Canvas creates/destroys drawing contexts via the Page/Factory; Canvas.getContext must cache the created context and retain a parent link so drawing APIs can reference the owning Canvas element.

Anchor delegates href resolution and component manipulation to the URL utilities and uses the Page base URL when resolving relative hrefs; its getters/setters map attribute changes into resolved values and must keep rel/relList consistency.
notable_relationships: Page (external to this folder): element attribute mutations and lifecycle callback invocations use Page mutation APIs and page._attribute_lookup; modifications here will affect navigation, event dispatch and CDP-visible state.

V8/ScriptManager and snapshot workflow: JsApi registrations and prototype shapes are consumed by V8 and included in snapshots — changing them may require snapshot regeneration and re-running build targets that produce snapshot.bin.

CustomElementRegistry (in html subfolder/registry): custom element upgrade and callback invocation coordinated across Html.zig and Custom.zig; lifecycle semantics here must remain consistent with the registry implementation.
URL utilities module: Anchor element helpers rely on the URL module and the Page base URL for resolution — changes to anchor behavior can affect link resolution across navigation and tests.

Drawing context factories and possibly native bindings: Canvas interacts with graphics context implementations; changes can affect rendering, OffscreenCanvas transfers, and memory usage tracked by CI benchmarks.
recommendations: Preserve the per-Page attribute identity lookup and ensure any attribute creation/cloning passes through Attribute.zig helpers so page._attribute_lookup remains correct.
When editing JsApi/prototype/constructor registration, be aware of V8 snapshot implications: snapshot regeneration and full test/smoke runs (including any snapshot build targets) may be required.
Keep canvas context creation idempotent and spec-compliant: maintain caching and parent linkage so object identity (ctx === canvas.getContext("2d")) and single-context locking is preserved.
Respect DOMStringMap SSO behavior and conversion utilities; do not introduce silent allocation/encoding changes that would alter property enumeration or break reversible camel/kebab transforms.

Ensure attribute setters/getters and element wrappers emit the same Page mutation notifications and keep auxiliary mappings (body.onload aliasing, rel vs relList, dir/lang/title delegation) synchronized.

Run formatting (zig build fmt), unit tests (make test / zig build test), and relevant HTML runner tests after changes; for JsApi or snapshot-impacting edits, run the snapshot creation build and any higher-level end-to-end tests that exercise V8 interactions.

src/browser/webapi/navigation.description: This folder implements the Navigation Web API surface and in-memory session history model used by pages: types, history entries, activation events and the Navigation controller that schedules, commits and dispatches navigations. It is the authoritative place for navigation semantics (push/replace/traverse/reload), the JS-visible history entry objects, and the transition metadata that other runtime components rely on during navigations.
key_behaviors: Manage an ordered session history as NavigationHistoryEntry objects and provide indexed/keyed access to entries from JS.
Drive navigations via promise-based APIs: navigate -> schedule on Page -> commitNavigation, with lifecycle events emitted for current-entry changes.
Create and expose NavigationActivation objects representing the activation event (activated entry, source entry, navigation type) to scripts.
Serialize/deserialize navigation state (JSON -> js.Value) on demand for JS consumers while keeping native state (id/key/url/index) authoritative.
Classify and propagate navigation metadata using the shared NavigationType/NavigationKind/NavigationTransition types for consistent handling across flows.

insights.title: Canonical session history ownership and mutation timing
insights.problem: History entries visible to JS can diverge from the internal session history if updates are not applied atomically, or events are emitted before internal indices/keys are settled.
insights.root_cause: Navigation methods mutate internal history (push/replace/updateEntries) and then asynchronously schedule Page work; JS-visible objects lazily convert state or read indices that may change during concurrent navigations.
insights.solution: Treat the Navigation controller as the canonical owner of entry id/index/key; ensure mutations and event dispatch order guarantee JS sees a consistent snapshot (atomic update semantics) or document that values are eventually consistent. Changes that affect entry identity or ordering require coordinated updates where events are emitted only after internal state is committed.

insights.title: JS-visible objects reference native structures with lifetimes
