insights.problem: NavigationHistoryEntry and NavigationActivation are exposed to scripts and may hold references to native state that can become stale if the underlying entry is removed or replaced.
insights.root_cause: JsApi bridge exposes accessors that convert or reference native state lazily, without a strict ownership/expiry model tied to history mutation/cancellation.
insights.solution: Edits must preserve or update the lifetime/invalidity semantics: either ensure bridged objects remain valid until GC or provide clear invalidation semantics. When changing data representation or accessors, update both native lifetime management and the JS accessors so scripts cannot observe deallocated or inconsistent data.

insights.title: Concurrent navigations, scheduling and promise resolution
insights.problem: Concurrent navigation requests (navigate/back/forward/reload/traverse) may race, leading to out-of-order commits, unresolved promises, or incorrect activation events.
insights.root_cause: Navigation schedules work on Page (asynchronously) and returns promises; without explicit locking, parallel invocations can interleave updateEntries/commitNavigation and event emission logic.
insights.solution: Any changes that touch navigate/navigateInner/pushEntry/commitNavigation must respect the existing scheduling/cancellation contract with Page. Maintain or explicitly update locking/cancellation semantics so promises are resolved/rejected consistently and activation events map to the correct committed entry.

insights.title: Navigation types and transition encoding are shared primitives
insights.problem: Multiple consumers rely on NavigationType, NavigationKind and NavigationTransition shapes; changing them without synchronized updates causes mismatches in callers or event consumers.
insights.root_cause: root.zig defines canonical types used by Navigation, NavigationActivation and callers elsewhere (event dispatch, telemetry, CDP mapping).
insights.solution: Treat root.zig declarations as a stable contract. Any edits to types or field encodings require coordinated updates across all consumers and tests; version the change if it alters wire/event semantics.

insights.title: On-demand JSON state parsing and correctness
insights.problem: Entry state is stored as JSON and parsed on demand; changes to parsing/formatting can produce different js.Value shapes or runtime errors when scripts read state.
insights.root_cause: NavigationHistoryEntry converts stored JSON to js.Value lazily via JsApi bridge; inconsistency in JSON handling or unknown edge cases (null, large structures) can surface at JS runtime.
insights.solution: When modifying state storage or parsing behavior ensure backward-compatible JSON semantics are preserved. Tests exercising getState and round-trip state persistence should be run after edits.

key_files.why: Central controller for navigation flows: mutation of history, scheduling navigations on Page, promise-returning API and event dispatch — changes here affect runtime navigation semantics.
Represents the JS-visible history entry and performs JSON->js.Value conversion; it is the bridge for state exposure and indexing semantics.
Encapsulates activation event payloads (activated entry, source entry, type) dispatched to scripts — shape matters for subscribers and event consumers.
Defines the shared NavigationType/Kind/State/Transition primitives that are the authoritative schema for navigation metadata throughout the runtime.

construct_relationships: Navigation (Navigation.zig) owns and mutates the session history composed of NavigationHistoryEntry objects and exposes operations (pushEntry, replaceEntry, updateEntries, commitNavigation).
Navigation schedules asynchronous navigation work on Page (external) and must resolve or reject the promise returned by navigate/navigateInner when Page commits or cancels the navigation.
NavigationActivation objects reference NavigationHistoryEntry instances (activated entry and optional source entry) and carry a NavigationType from root.zig to describe the activation reason.
NavigationHistoryEntry lazily converts stored JSON state into a js.Value via the JsApi bridge so script accessors return JS-native state representations.
root.zig provides NavigationType/NavigationKind/NavigationTransition definitions that are consumed by Navigation, NavigationActivation and by event emission code to encode navigation metadata.
notable_relationships: Page scheduling and lifecycle (src/browser/Page.zig) — Navigation delegates actual network/load/commit work to Page; any change to navigation scheduling must be reconciled with Page's expectations and cancellation semantics.
JS bridge / V8 integration (ScriptManager / JsApi) — Navigation and entry accessors are exposed to script contexts; changes can require snapshot regeneration or affect JS-visible interfaces.

Event-routing / CDP surfaces (mcp/Server.zig / cdp domain mappings) — activation events and history changes are emitted to clients; shape/stability of event payloads impacts external tools implementing Playwright/Puppeteer compatibility.

Network and URL resolution (src/network/http.zig and URL helpers) — navigation decisions (reload, traverse, new url) intersect with URL resolution and network fetch logic; history indices and commit semantics assume consistent URL canonicalization.
Persistence / serialization (if used elsewhere) — id/key generation and state JSON handling may be relied upon by higher-level session storage or debugging tools.
recommendations: Preserve Navigation API binary and JS-facing contract: if changing function signatures, types, or exported fields, update all consumers (Page, event emitters, CDP mappings) and consider snapshot regeneration when JS bindings change.

When editing navigation flows, validate promise lifecycle semantics: run unit tests and integration tests that exercise concurrent navigations, cancellation, back/forward traversal, and reload to catch race conditions.

If changing root.zig types (NavigationType/Kind/Transition), update all callers and event serialization code; run any CDP/event compatibility tests to ensure external clients receive the expected payload shapes.

For changes that touch state serialization or NavigationHistoryEntry.getState, run tests that assert JSON round-trip and JS-visible shape correctness (including edge cases like null, nested structures, and large payloads).

Respect lifetime/invalidation semantics of JS-exposed objects: ensure bridged objects remain valid for the expected duration or implement explicit invalidation; coordinate with ScriptManager/V8 to avoid use-after-free bugs.

Run format and build checks (zig build fmt and zig build) and the project test suite (zig build test or make test). For changes that affect runtime navigation behavior, run end-to-end serve + CDP interaction flows (make end2end or the demo scripts) to validate external compatibility.

src/browser/webapi/canvas.description: This folder implements browser-side Canvas and WebGL web APIs exposed to JavaScript via the engine bridge. It provides in-process 2D canvas contexts (onscreen and offscreen), ImageData handling, and a minimal WebGL extension shim; most actual rendering is stubbed and the focus is on API surface compatibility, data buffer management, and integration with the page/JS object factory.
key_behaviors: Create and expose Canvas/OffscreenCanvas and their 2D contexts to JS (JsApi bridge) so scripts can call getContext and access context properties.
Parse and apply simple fillStyle values and create/return ImageData buffers to and from JS code (createImageData, getImageData, putImageData).
Provide OffscreenCanvas Promise-based blob export (convertToBlob) and a stubbed transferToImageBitmap API surface.
Expose a WebGLRenderingContext facade for extension enumeration and enabling (getExtension, getSupportedExtensions) without full GPU/WebGL pipeline.
Integrate constructed ImageData/Blob/Promise objects with the Page-level object factory and JS runtime wrappers so they are accessible from scripts.

insights.title: Many drawing operations are stubs
insights.problem: Most drawing methods are no-ops; consumers expecting rendering effects will not observe visual output.
insights.root_cause: The implementation focuses on API surface and data interchange (ImageData/Blob) rather than full raster/GPU rendering.
insights.solution: Treat this module as providing logical API compatibility only; callers/tests should not depend on pixel-accurate rendering. Any change must preserve existing stub semantics unless full rendering is intentionally added.

insights.title: Canvas accessor must reference element
insights.problem: ctx.canvas was previously unimplemented so scripts could not get back to the source canvas element.
insights.root_cause: The accessor/back-reference linking between context and its canvas element was missing in the binding layer.
insights.solution: Ensure context instances hold and expose a stable reference to their originating canvas/OffscreenCanvas via the JsApi wrapper and that lifecycle/ownership of that reference is consistent with other DOM objects.

insights.title: ImageData buffer ownership and exposure
insights.problem: ImageData creation/get operations allocate and return buffers to JS; incorrect lifecycle handling can cause memory corruption or leaks.
insights.root_cause: ImageData backing storage bridges Zig-managed buffers into the JS runtime and must respect transfer/copy semantics and GC boundaries.
insights.solution: When modifying ImageData creation/get/put code, verify buffer allocation strategy, copy vs. shared semantics, and the JS wrapper's finalization behavior so buffers are not freed while JS still holds references.

insights.title: convertToBlob returns a placeholder Blob
insights.problem: convertToBlob currently returns an empty Blob Promise and transferToImageBitmap is unimplemented; callers expecting image data will receive empty results.
insights.root_cause: Full raster export/encoding is not implemented; API exists to match surface but not content production.
insights.solution: Keep Promise resolution and Blob object creation patterns compatible with the rest of the codebase; tests and consumers should be aware of empty/stubbed payloads and handle accordingly.

insights.title: Extensions-only WebGL shim
insights.problem: WebGLRenderingContext only mimics extension enumeration/enabling, not WebGL rendering.
insights.root_cause: The folder provides a façade for extension discovery to satisfy scripts that probe for capabilities, without implementing the full WebGL spec.
insights.solution: Ensure extension names and returned wrapper objects match expected strings/behaviors; do not assume GLES/WebGL context state is available.

key_files.why: Primary onscreen 2D context implementation; exposes fillStyle, ImageData creation/get semantics and the ctx.canvas accessor to JS.
Constructs OffscreenCanvas instances, returns 2D contexts, and implements convertToBlob Promise API surface used by worker/Offscreen code.
Offscreen variant of the 2D context which integrates with the page factory and mirrors ImageData and fillStyle behavior for offscreen usage.
WebGL façade focused on extension discovery and enabling; relevant where scripts probe for WebGL capability or expect extension objects.

construct_relationships: CanvasRenderingContext2D and OffscreenCanvasRenderingContext2D both implement fillStyle parsing and ImageData create/get/put functionality so they must share consistent buffer semantics.
OffscreenCanvas creates and returns OffscreenCanvasRenderingContext2D instances via getContext(), linking the OffscreenCanvas JsApi wrapper to its context object for back-reference semantics.

CanvasRenderingContext2D exposes a 'canvas' accessor that returns the originating canvas element; this back-reference must map to the same JsApi/Dom object instance provided by the canvas/OffscreenCanvas constructor.

ImageData and Blob objects created here are produced via the Page-level object factory (integration point) so the JsApi wrappers, lifetimes, and finalizers must align with the page's object management.
WebGLRenderingContext exposes extension objects (debug renderer info, lose context) which are small JS wrapper constructs that follow the same JsApi exposure pattern used by the 2D contexts.
notable_relationships: Page factory and DOM binding code (outside this folder) are downstream consumers/providers of ImageData, Blob, and context objects; changes here can require coordinated updates to the page/JsApi glue so object wrappers remain valid.

The JS bridge (JsApi) and V8 snapshot/wrapping conventions are upstream constraints: altering public signatures or wrapper shapes may require snapshot regeneration or changes in the ScriptManager/V8 integration.

Higher-level browser flows (navigation, serialization, CDP events) may rely on these APIs for scripts; since rendering is stubbed, end-to-end tests or clients that rely on visual output will behave differently.

Promise and Blob semantics here must interoperate with other modules that create or consume Promises/Blob types; inconsistent construction patterns will affect script compatibility and test expectations.
recommendations: Preserve existing JsApi shapes and object identity semantics: any change to constructors, accessor names, or wrapper internals can break JS-level references and must be synchronized with the V8 binding/snapshot if applicable.

When editing ImageData/Blob buffer allocation or transfer code, validate memory ownership, copy vs shared semantics, and finalizer behavior to prevent use-after-free or leaks. Run unit tests that exercise getImageData/putImageData and any GC-related tests.

Do not assume rendering side effects: tests or callers should be expected to receive stubbed or empty pixel/Blob data unless a rendering implementation is explicitly added. Ensure convertToBlob continues to return a Promise-resolved Blob object even if empty.

If you change or add WebGL extension names or wrapper objects, verify that scripts that probe getSupportedExtensions/getExtension still receive the expected strings/objects. Update any tests that assert exact extension lists.

Run formatting and build checks (zig build fmt, zig build) and the project's unit tests (make test) after edits. If changing JsApi shapes that are embedded in a snapshot, rebuild the V8 snapshot (snapshot_creator build) and ensure snapshots used by builds/tests are updated.

src/browser/webapi/selector.description:

src/browser/webapi/media.description: This folder implements Web Media-related WebAPI bindings exposed to the embedded JavaScript engine: a MediaError object and WebVTT cue types (generic TextTrackCue and concrete VTTCue). These types provide JavaScript-visible constructors, constants, property accessors, and event/callback integration so media elements and text-track consumers inside pages can create, inspect and react to media errors and timed text cues. They matter because they form the surface area scripts rely on for media error handling and caption/subtitle timing/event behavior, and they must interoperate correctly with the engine's JS bridge, event system and page lifecycle.
key_behaviors: Creation and exposure of JS-visible media objects (MediaError, TextTrackCue, VTTCue) via the JsApi bridge so page scripts can construct and inspect them.
Providing spec-aligned constants and accessors (MEDIA_ERR_* codes, cue start/end times, text, layout attributes) that remain stable when read/written from JavaScript.
Event/callback lifecycle for cues: storing onenter/onexit handlers, integrating with EventTarget so cues can dispatch enter/exit events and invoke script callbacks.
Prototype and inheritance mapping: VTTCue maps into the TextTrackCue prototype to present concrete behavior while sharing generic timing/event machinery.
Placeholder or unimplemented features accounted for (e.g., getCueAsHTML) which callers may expect but currently are no-ops or stubs.

insights.title: JsApi exposure must remain stable and spec-conformant
insights.problem: These files expose constructors, constants and accessors to scripts; consumers (pages, tests, CDP clients) expect consistent names, behaviors and error codes.
insights.root_cause: Tight coupling between Zig-side object shapes and V8-visible prototypes; small signature or naming changes can break runtime scripts or external automation expecting exact APIs.
insights.solution: When editing these files, preserve public JsApi names, property semantics and MEDIA_ERR_* numerical values; any change requires updating corresponding tests and any CDP/automation expectations.

insights.title: Cue event callbacks introduce reentrancy and lifetime constraints
insights.problem: TextTrackCue stores onenter/onexit handlers and integrates with EventTarget; invoking these callbacks can re-enter page scripts and interact with DOM or cue lifetimes.
insights.root_cause: Callbacks are held as JS-visible handles and may be invoked from internal event loops or network/parse callbacks leading to reentrancy and ownership issues.
insights.solution: Edits must respect callback ownership, ensure safe invocation contexts (event loop / task queue), and avoid invalidating cue objects while callbacks may run. Consider implications for garbage collection / reference lifetimes managed by the JS bridge.

insights.title: Unimplemented or placeholder methods (getCueAsHTML) are observable
insights.problem: VTTCue includes a placeholder getCueAsHTML that is not implemented; callers or tests may rely on it or expect a fallback behaviour.
insights.root_cause: Partial implementation of the WebVTT API surface where only core properties were implemented initially.
insights.solution: Treat these methods as part of the public surface: any change (implementing, removing, or altering behavior) can affect callers and tests, so coordinate updates with tests or document the placeholder status clearly to consumers.

insights.title: Media error code values must match web platform expectations
insights.problem: MediaError exposes MEDIA_ERR_* constants; mismatched numeric values would break script checks and spec compliance.
insights.root_cause: Hard-coded numeric constants on the Zig side must match the web spec and any tests that assert these values.
insights.solution: Ensure numeric constants remain unchanged unless intentionally updated with synchronized test/consumers updates; validate against existing tests that assert these codes.

key_files.why: Defines the MediaError object, its MEDIA_ERR_* constants and JS-visible accessors — used by media elements and scripts to represent media load/play errors.
Implements the generic TextTrackCue structure, timing fields and onenter/onexit callback storage and EventTarget integration that underpins all cue types.
Provides the concrete VTTCue implementation mapping WebVTT-specific properties into the TextTrackCue prototype for caption/subtitle presentation.

construct_relationships: VTTCue in VTTCue.zig composes/extends TextTrackCue behavior from TextTrackCue.zig: it uses the generic timing and event/callback machinery while adding text/position/layout properties.
TextTrackCue holds JS callback references (onenter/onexit) and integrates with the EventTarget implementation elsewhere in the runtime to dispatch cue enter/exit events.

MediaError exposes constant values and constructors via the JsApi bridge; scripts obtain instances to inspect code and message, and code consumers expect MEDIA_ERR_* constants to be present on the JS-facing prototype.
All three files rely on the JsApi exposure conventions — naming, prototype registration and getter/setter signatures — to be consumable by the V8 ScriptManager layer.
notable_relationships: These WebAPI objects are consumed by media elements and text-track logic elsewhere in the browser runtime (page, element and track management code), so changes can affect navigation/dump flows and CDP-exposed state.

The JsApi exposure depends on the global JS binding and snapshot setup from ScriptManager/V8; modifications may impact snapshot generation or runtime bindings if constructor names or property layouts change.

Event dispatch for cues ties into the central event loop and EventTarget implementation (outside this folder); edits must respect the event queueing and task ordering conventions used by the rest of the codebase.
Tests and automation tooling (end-to-end demos, CDP clients) may validate these APIs; any change can ripple into CI e2e jobs and demo scripts.
recommendations: Preserve public JsApi names, property names, and MEDIA_ERR_* numeric values unless coordinated updates to tests and consumers are planned.
When touching callback storage/invocation, ensure invocation happens in the correct event/task context and does not violate object lifetimes or cause reentrancy-related use-after-free issues.
Be aware of V8 snapshot and binding implications: renaming constructors/prototypes may require regenerating snapshots and rebuilding artifacts consumed by release builds.
Run code formatting and unit tests after edits (zig build fmt; zig build test / make test) and, where applicable, run end-to-end/demo or CDP checks that exercise media/cue behavior.
Treat placeholder methods (e.g., getCueAsHTML) as part of the public surface — document or synchronize changes with consumers and tests to avoid unexpected breakage.

src/browser/webapi/storage.description: This folder implements web storage primitives used by pages and the network layer: an in-memory cookie jar (parsing, normalization, matching and lifetime management) and per-origin key/value storage (local/session) with JS bindings. Both modules enforce web security and quota rules and are surfaced to page JavaScript contexts, so correctness here affects request authorization, privacy boundaries, and deterministic page behavior.
key_behaviors: Parse and validate Set-Cookie headers, normalize attributes (domain, path, expires/max-age, secure, sameSite) and persist cookies into an in-memory Jar with quota and size checks.
Select and serialize cookies applicable to an outgoing HTTP request according to domain/path/secure/sameSite rules and cookie expiration.
Provide per-origin storage buckets (local/session) with get/set/remove/clear operations, enforce size/entry quotas, and expose these operations to page JS via a JS API bridge.
Manage origin-scoped storage metadata (Shed/Bucket lifecycle) and integrate with the page arena so storage can be accessed by executing scripts.

insights.title: Public Suffix List (PSL) enforcement gap
insights.problem: Set-Cookie domain parsing allowed cookies to be set on public suffixes (e.g., .co.uk), which can let unrelated sites set cookies that match many origins and leak scope.
insights.root_cause: parseDomain accepted multi-level public suffixes without correctly consulting or enforcing PSL rules when deciding if a domain is a registrable domain or a public suffix.
insights.solution: Ensure domain validation rejects public suffixes when setting cookies and correctly treats effective top-level domains per PSL/RFC rules; changes here will alter which cookies are stored and matched.

insights.title: Loose handling of empty domains/hosts
insights.problem: Cookies with empty domain fields or empty-host URLs were accepted/created in ways that could result in cookies that match too broadly or are associated with invalid origins.
insights.root_cause: Default-domain and empty domain field logic was permissive and did not strictly validate empty hostnames or enforce origin-based defaulting rules.
insights.solution: Apply explicit empty-host and default-domain rules so cookies created without a domain attribute are tied to the request host and reject or normalize cookies when the request URL has an empty host.

insights.title: Per-origin quota and JS exposure
insights.problem: Storage Bucket operations enforce quotas and expose methods to JS; mistakes in quota enforcement or serialization can cause runtime errors visible to page scripts or inconsistent persisted state.
insights.root_cause: Tight coupling of quota checks, arena allocation, and JS bridge means an error in one place (for example, size calculation or serialization) impacts JS-visible APIs and may leak memory or corrupt Bucket state.
insights.solution: Treat Bucket quota checks, serialization boundaries, and JS API marshalling as atomic and consistent operations; when editing, preserve how sizes are computed and how Bucket lifetimes are tied to the page arena.

insights.title: Tests and runtime integration expectations
insights.problem: Storage includes an embedded test-runner invocation and cookies have nuanced matching semantics; changes risk regressions that only surface in integration or E2E tests.
insights.root_cause: Behavioral correctness depends on interactions with network/request code and script execution; unit tests in this folder cover only a subset of integration concerns.
insights.solution: When modifying code, run both the unit tests in this folder and integration/end-to-end tests that exercise navigation, HTTP requests (cookie headers), and JS storage APIs so behavioral contracts are validated.

key_files.why: Implements cookie parsing, normalization, the in-memory Jar, matching logic for outgoing requests and enforces security rules (domain/path/sameSite/secure).
Provides per-origin storage buckets, quota enforcement, lifecycle management (Shed/Bucket) and the JS API bindings used by page scripts.

construct_relationships: Cookie.parse / parseDomain produce Cookie objects that Jar.add persists and Jar.appliesTo / select logic uses to build Cookie headers for outgoing HTTP requests.
Jar maintains expiration and size/quota bookkeeping which is consulted on lookup and mutation operations to evict or reject cookies.
Shed organizes per-origin Bucket instances; Bucket provides get/set/remove/clear semantics and Lookup.JsApi marshals these operations into the page JS context.
Storage Bucket lifetimes are tied to the page arena and are expected to be stable for the duration of a page/session; JS bridge calls mutate Bucket state atomically with quota checks.
notable_relationships: Network layer (src/network/http.zig) is an upstream consumer of Cookie.Jar selection logic to attach Cookie headers to requests and will be affected by cookie matching/domain rules.

Page runtime and ScriptManager/V8 (src/browser/Page.zig and src/browser/ScriptManager.zig) invoke storage JS APIs exposed by storage.zig; changes to JS marshalling or object lifetimes can break script-visible behavior or snapshot assumptions.
CDP/server or page lifecycle logic in src/browser/Browser.zig may rely on storage and cookie semantics for operations like clearing site data, navigation isolation and session management.

HTML parsing and navigation flows (html5ever bridge) interact indirectly: navigation->network->cookies->DOM/script execution; editing storage/cookie logic can change deterministic outcomes of navigations and tests.
recommendations: Preserve RFC/PSL-based cookie validation semantics: any edits that touch domain parsing or matching must respect public suffix rules and default-domain behaviors.
Do not change quota/size calculation semantics without updating all places that rely on those values (Bucket size checks, eviction logic, and JS-visible errors).
Run the local unit tests for this folder and the repository-wide integration/end-to-end tests (make test, make end2end/demo flows) after changes that affect cookie matching or storage JS bindings.

When modifying Cookie.zig, validate interactions with src/network/http.zig (cookie header generation) and relevant request tests; when modifying storage.zig, validate with Page/ScriptManager JS execution tests and any snapshot-created behavior.

Respect arena/lifetime ownership conventions: Bucket lifetime and cookie Jar storage are tied into page/session lifecycles—ensure no dangling references or inconsistent freeing across page navigations and session closures.
Keep API surface and error semantics stable for JS bindings: changes to Lookup.JsApi marshaling may be visible to user scripts and to V8 snapshot behavior used in builds.

src/cdp/domains.description: This folder implements the per-domain handlers for the Chrome DevTools Protocol (CDP) surface exposed by Lightpanda. Each file maps CDP domain methods/events to internal browser runtime operations (pages, network transfers, cookie jar, node registry, inspector) and serializes CDP events/responses for external clients — it is the protocol bridge that must remain compatible with CDP expectations used by tools like Puppeteer/Playwright.
key_behaviors: Translate incoming CDP commands into concrete operations on BrowserContext, Page, Cookie.Jar, Transfer, node registry, and inspector components
Emit CDP domain events in client-expected order and with the required payload shapes (e.g., Page lifecycle events, Network request/response events, DOM.setChildNodes)
Register and expose DOM nodes via backendNodeId and resolve node-related queries (selectors, box model, quads, accessibility trees) through the node registry
Manage request interception and Fetch domain flows: pause/resume/fulfill/continue requests and handle auth challenges by manipulating internal Transfer/HttpClient state
Present minimal/no-op implementations for many domains (Emulation, CSS, Log, Performance, Inspector) to satisfy client compatibility while delegating real work to core subsystems where needed

insights.title: Domain handlers must accept concrete CDP command types
insights.problem: Several domain handlers historically used anytype/generic command parameters which led to inconsistent imports and fragile compile-time behavior.
insights.root_cause: Handlers relied on generic/anytype signatures and ad-hoc imports, causing mismatches after refactors and making it easy to pass incorrectly-typed Command instances.
insights.solution: Domain functions should use concrete *CDP.Command signatures and consistent imports; when editing domain files, ensure command parameter types match CDP.Command and reconcile any changed function signatures across router/caller sites to avoid type mismatches.

insights.title: Node registration logic is centralized but was previously duplicated
insights.problem: Interactive element discovery and other domain handlers duplicated node registration logic (creating separate nodeIds arrays), risking inconsistent backendNodeId assignment and lifecycle handling.
insights.root_cause: Multiple domain implementations re-implemented node registration instead of using a single registry API; changes in one spot didn't propagate to others.
insights.solution: Respect the central node registry and its registration lifecycle: reuse its registration path and semantics when returning backendNodeId and when emitting DOM/AX responses. When modifying code that registers nodes, update all call sites that assume particular backendNodeId lifetimes or arrays.

insights.title: CDP event ordering must match client expectations
insights.problem: Page navigation and lifecycle events were emitted in a different order than Chrome, causing client-visible differences (e.g., page_navigated emitted too late, missing JS dialog events).
insights.root_cause: Event emission was tied to internal lifecycle milestones without mapping to the exact order CDP clients expect.
insights.solution: Preserve the expected CDP ordering for Page.* events (frameNavigated, domContentEventFired, loadEventFired, lifecycle events) and emit dialog-opening events; when adjusting navigation or lifecycle code, validate event sequences against client expectations (Puppeteer/Chrome ordering).

insights.title: Request serialization must include Cookie header when emitting Network events
insights.problem: Transfer->CDP serialization omitted the Cookie header for requests reconstructed from Transfer objects, making Network.requestWillBeSent payloads incomplete for clients expecting request headers.
insights.root_cause: Serialization path didn't include Cookie header when building the CDP request representation from internal Transfer structures.
insights.solution: Ensure Transfer->CDP serialization includes the Cookie header and other headers clients rely on; when editing Network or Transfer serialization, check that cookie-related fields are present and that cookie policies from BrowserContext are honored.

insights.title: Request interception requires careful Transfer/resume semantics
insights.problem: Fetch domain pauses and resumes requests via an InterceptState and translates CDP continue/fulfill actions to internal Transfer operations; mismatches in types (http.Method/Header types) or state handling break interception flows.
insights.root_cause: Domain code and internal HTTP types diverged (net_http -> http) and interception state lifecycle is tightly coupled to Transfer/I/O state.
insights.solution: When updating fetch/interception code, preserve the InterceptState semantics, use the canonical http types (http.Header, http.Method), and ensure resume/fulfill/continue flows correctly map to underlying Transfer operations and auth handling.

key_files.why: Primary implementation of Page domain: navigation lifecycle, script injection, isolated worlds, and the ordering of Page events expected by CDP clients.
Exposes DOM structure, selector search, node resolution and setChildNodes events — central to node registry, selector machinery, and client-side DOM introspection.
Handles Network.* commands and emits request/response/loading events; integrates with BrowserContext cookie jar and Transfer serialization used by clients.
Implements request interception semantics (pause/continue/fulfill/auth) and maps CDP Fetch actions to internal Transfer/HttpClient behavior — delicate stateful logic.
Lightpanda-specific domain exposing page analysis and action primitives (semantic tree, markdown, interactive elements) and responsible for backend node registration used by tools.

construct_relationships: Page domain handlers call Browser/Page runtime APIs to initiate navigations, create isolated worlds, execute reloads, and register scripts to run on new documents.
DOM domain uses the node registry and selector machinery to implement getDocument, performSearch, querySelector(s), resolveNode and dispatches DOM.setChildNodes events back to clients.

Network domain interacts with BrowserContext cookie jar and Transfer objects to emit Network.requestWillBeSent/responseReceived/loadingFinished/loadingFailed events and to service cookie methods via Storage/Network get/set/delete.
Fetch domain manipulates Transfer and InterceptState to pause HTTP transfers and translate CDP continue/fulfill/continueWithAuth commands into HttpClient/Transfer operations.
Runtime domain forwards runtime/evaluate and callFunctionOn to the browser inspector; inspector responses are passed back as CDP Runtime results.
notable_relationships: This subtree is invoked by the CDP router/server (src/mcp/Server.zig) which dispatches incoming websocket messages to these domain processMessage handlers — changing signatures or expected Command types requires updating the router.

Domain handlers depend on core runtime modules: src/browser/Browser.zig, src/browser/Page.zig, src/network/http.zig, node registry and Cookie.Jar; changes here often require coordinating with those modules.

Many domains provide compatibility shims (no-op implementations) to satisfy external clients; removing or changing these responses can break client attach/enable flows (Puppeteer/Playwright expect these domains to succeed).

Event emission and payload shapes are consumed by external CDP clients and test suites (end-to-end/demo, WPT); modifications must preserve client-observed semantics and ordering to avoid breaking integrations and CI tests.
recommendations: Do not change processMessage function signatures without updating the CDP router and all callers — handlers are expected to accept concrete *CDP.Command types.

When modifying node registration or backendNodeId logic, verify every domain that constructs node lists (DOM, Accessibility, LP, Interactive) uses the same registry API and lifetime rules to avoid inconsistent backend IDs.

Preserve CDP event ordering and payload shapes, especially for Page lifecycle events and Network request/response events — run integration tests (make test, demo end-to-end) to validate observable sequencing.
If altering network serialization, ensure Cookie header and other request headers are included when building CDP Network.requestWillBeSent payloads and validate against clients that inspect headers.
When changing Fetch/interception behavior, respect InterceptState lifecycles and use canonical http types (http.Header/http.Method). Test pause/resume/fulfill/auth flows with real HTTP transfers.
Keep no-op domain handlers intact (Emulation, CSS, Log, Performance, Inspector) unless you coordinate with client behavior tests — external clients rely on successful enable/disable semantics.

Run formatter and unit tests after edits (zig build fmt; make test). For changes affecting client integration semantics, run end-to-end/demo tests and relevant CI workflows to catch compatibility regressions.

src/browser/js.description: This folder contains the Zig-side V8 embedding: isolate/context lifecycle, the Zig↔V8 bridge, per-context/local helpers, identity and origin scoping, and DevTools inspector integration. It centralizes how native types/functions/state are exposed in JS, how modules and promises are compiled/instantiated/persisted, and how V8 task pumping and memory events are delivered to the browser runtime — all of which are critical for correct execution, memory safety, and cross-context identity guarantees.
key_behaviors: Create and manage a V8 Isolate and its global templates (Env) and pump V8 micro/macrotasks and platform message loop across contexts.
Create/destroy per-page V8 Contexts, map Zig Page/session state into V8 contexts, and provide per-context arenas and handle scopes.
Provide a compile-time-driven bridge that defines native constructors, functions, properties and accessors, marshals arguments/results, and maps errors into JS exceptions.
Maintain session/identity mappings and origin-scoped tokens so Zig instances map 1:1 to persistent JS wrappers across same-origin contexts and IsolatedWorlds.
Compile/instantiate/evaluate/persist ES modules and manage persistent handles (Temp/Global) for functions, objects, promises and modules across context lifetimes.
Expose V8 Inspector sessions and wrap JS objects into RemoteObjects for DevTools/CDP integration.

insights.title: TAO and origin-scoped allocations must outlive contexts
insights.problem: TaggedOpaque (TAO) objects and other origin-scoped allocations were being allocated in per-context arenas and freed when a context was destroyed, causing dangling references in other contexts of the same origin.
insights.root_cause: Context-level arenas were used for allocations that logically belong to origin/session scope and must survive context teardown.
insights.solution: Allocate TAOs and other origin-shared objects in the identity/origin arena (session-level) so they persist across context destruction; ensure mappings/cleanups run at the identity/origin lifetime boundary.

insights.title: Finalizer model must be explicit and session-coordinated
insights.problem: Finalizers and temporary/global handle tracking were fragmented (stored on Identity/Context inconsistently) and used weak/strong boolean models that didn't compose across prototype chains or IsolatedWorlds, producing misbalanced acquire/release, hidden leaks, or use-after-free.
insights.root_cause: Mixed ownership semantics across Local/Context/Identity and implicit finalizer behavior made correct lifetime bookkeeping brittle.
insights.solution: Use explicit acquire/release (RC) semantics with session-level finalizer and handle registries; store finalizer metadata and persistent handles at session/identity scope and ensure every acquire has a matching release within the same session semantics.

insights.title: Bridge property/accessor metadata must match runtime behavior
insights.problem: Binding metadata lacked flags (e.g., deletable) and omitted registrations (e.g., FormDataEvent), and there were unused compile-time helpers present, causing inconsistent property descriptors and missing JS types.
insights.root_cause: Compile-time binding definitions diverged from runtime needs; unused generic helpers were retained.
insights.solution: Ensure Builder/Constructor/Accessor metadata includes all descriptor flags required at definition time (deletable, configurable, etc.) and that all JS-exposed types are registered in the bridge; remove dead helpers to avoid confusion about lifecycle semantics.

insights.title: Env must reliably manage V8 micro/macrotasks and memory signals
insights.problem: Microtask and macrotask draining, ms-to-next-macrotask calculation, and memory pressure notification must be coherent across contexts; failures produce stalled promises, missed background tasks, or missed low-memory handling.
insights.root_cause: Isolate-level task scheduling responsibilities were distributed across contexts without a single owner ensuring timely pumping and memory notifications.
insights.solution: Keep Env responsible for runMicrotasks/runMacrotasks/pumpMessageLoop and for signaling memory pressure; changes to context creation/destruction must preserve these semantics and timing guarantees.

insights.title: Identity map must preserve 1:1 mapping and be cleaned at correct scope
insights.problem: Identity mappings were sometimes stored/cleared at the wrong scope resulting in duplicated wrappers or orphaned persistent handles.
insights.root_cause: Storage and cleanup responsibilities for identity_map were split between Context, Identity and Session in inconsistent ways.
insights.solution: Centralize identity map ownership and cleanup at session/identity lifetime; when editing code that creates or clears persistent V8 handles, ensure that clear/deinit runs at the matching identity/session boundary and that Contexts consult the centralized map.

key_files.why: Isolate lifecycle, snapshot/template initialization, context pool management, and the implementation of micro/macrotask & message loop pumping — changes here affect all contexts and runtime behavior.
Per-V8-context mapping, module compile/instantiate/evaluate logic, per-context arenas and identity interaction — central to context lifecycle and module caching.
Defines how Zig types/functions/properties are exposed to JS; builders and metadata here determine constructor and accessor behavior across the embedding.
Provides the conversion layer and compilation/execution helpers used by callers to create JS values, compile scripts, and manage identity mapping in an executing context.
Contains session-level identity map and persistent-handle cleanup; changes here affect object identity, lifetime and cross-context wrapper reuse.

construct_relationships: Env creates and owns the V8 Isolate and global templates, and it creates/destroys Context instances; Env is responsible for task pumping (runMicrotasks/runMacrotasks/pumpMessageLoop) which drives Local/Context execution.

Context maps a v8::Context to Zig state and delegates per-call conversions and script compilation/execution to Local; Context holds module cache entries (Module) and uses Module to instantiate/evaluate ES modules.
Local contains helpers used by Context and Caller to convert Zig values to JS and to compile/run scripts; Local also maps Zig instances into JS wrappers via Identity and origin arenas.

Identity maintains the session-level map of Zig instances to v8::Global wrappers used by Local/Context to ensure 1:1 object identity across same-origin contexts; Origin provides security tokens and arenas that Identity/Context share.

bridge.zig supplies Builder metadata used by Env snapshot/global templates and by Context/Local to create constructors, functions and accessors; Caller uses that metadata to dispatch callbacks from V8 into typed Zig functions.
Function/Object/Promise wrappers use HandleScope and Isolate helpers to manage temporary handles and to persist Temp/Global references that are tracked by Identity/Env registries.

Inspector wraps Context objects into inspector sessions and RemoteObjects and forwards protocol messages into the embedding; Inspector depends on Context/Local identity and persistent handles to unwrap remote objects back into Zig.
notable_relationships: Env depends on snapshot data produced by the snapshot creator build target; changes to Env initialization or snapshot-templated globals may require regenerating snapshot.bin and updating build invocation.
Inspector integration feeds DevTools/CDP messages upstream to the mcp/CDP server layer; modifications to Inspector/RemoteObject shapes can affect the CDP mapping and client-visible DevTools behavior.

Module persistence and identity handling interact with higher-level Browser/Page code and the ScriptManager; edits here can change navigation/script execution semantics consumed by page lifecycle code.

Session/identity arenas are semantically shared with broader session management (Session, Browser) — edits that re-scope where persistent handles are stored can require coordinated changes in session-level teardown and finalizer registries.
recommendations: Preserve V8 handle/HandleScope semantics: every native callback must open an appropriate HandleScope and respect per-call arenas to avoid leaking V8 locals or referencing freed locals.

Keep TAOs, persistent Temp/Global handles and finalizer registries at identity/session scope (not per-context) so cross-context references remain valid; when changing allocation arenas, update matching deinit/cleanup codepaths.

When changing bridge metadata or Builder definitions, ensure Caller dispatch and property descriptor flags (e.g., deletable) are consistent and that all JS-exposed types are registered (FormDataEvent and similar) to avoid missing constructors at runtime.

Do not break Env's task-pumping contract: maintain runMicrotasks/runMacrotasks and pumpMessageLoop behaviors and memory-pressure notifications; any change that affects timing may impact promise resolution and background tasks.

Run formatting and the full test matrix after edits: zig build fmt and zig build test (unit), and exercise serve/fetch/dump flows (including CDP/Inspector flows). For changes to snapshot-templated globals or Env init, regenerate and use the snapshot build target and validate e2e/demo or CDP tests.

Respect single-threaded isolate model: modifications must not introduce cross-thread access to isolate-bound constructs; if introducing new registries, ensure they obey isolate affinity and are cleaned on Env deinit.

src/browser/webapi.description: This subtree implements the browser-facing Web API surface and DOM primitives used by page scripts: Document/Node/Range, the Window global, network-facing APIs (fetch/XHR), Blobs, AbortSignal/Controller, Crypto, Custom Elements, and small helpers (CSS, console, parser). It is the glue between the in-memory DOM, the Page/V8 bridge (JsApi), and native subsystems (network, page scheduler, arenas and reference-counting), so correctness here is critical for script-facing semantics, lifetime safety, and interoperability with other runtime components.
key_behaviors: Create, query and mutate an in-memory DOM (Document creates nodes, maintains ownerDocument links and id cache, import/adopt semantics, and drives live collections and ranges).
Expose and bind JS-visible Web APIs (Window, Console, Crypto, Blob, AbortController, CustomElementRegistry, DOMParser, DOMException) to V8 via JsApi and page-local arena allocation.

Bridge JavaScript-initiated network activity (fetch(), XMLHttpRequest) to the native HttpClient layer, including promise/event lifecycle, header/body accumulation, abort and timeout handling, and cookie origin propagation.

Manage per-page lifetimes and cross-language ownership: arena allocation model for short-lived data, page-level reference counting (lp.RC) for long-lived objects, and coordinated finalization/unregistering before release to avoid use-after-free.

Maintain DOM-derived invariants on mutations: update live ranges, NodeIterators/TreeWalkers, selection and observers (e.g., IntersectionObserver) in response to character-data/node insertion/removal and attribute changes.
Enforce web security and cross-origin boundaries on global/window accessors (CrossOriginWindow wrappers) and integrate navigation/storage rules where applicable.

insights.title: Reference-counting migration requires acquire/release ordering
insights.problem: Several webapi types previously used finalizers or numeric refcount fields; switching to lp.RC and acquire/release semantics left ambiguous finalization and exposed use-after-free bugs when unregistering occurred after release.
insights.root_cause: Mix of finalizer-based deinit and manual integer refcounting across modules meant some code released objects before unregistering them from page-level registries or callbacks.
insights.solution: Adopt explicit acquireRef/releaseRef ordering: unregister or unlink references before calling releaseRef, and prefer RC-based lifetimes across webapi types (Blobs, Ranges, IntersectionObserver entries, etc.).

insights.title: Fetch/XHR must carry cookie origin and use unified Response/header iterator plumbing
insights.problem: Fetch and XMLHttpRequest implementations did not propagate cookie_origin or used outdated header accumulation APIs, breaking SameSite cookie behavior and leaving TODOs for header iteration.
insights.root_cause: Rework of the HttpClient/Response abstraction and centralization of cookie handling changed API shapes; callers were not updated consistently to the new header iterator and cookie_origin contract.
insights.solution: Ensure fetch/XHR populate cookie_origin (page URL) on outgoing requests and use the unified Response/header iterator callbacks to build Response/XHR headers/body consistently; honor AbortSignal and native timeouts.

insights.title: Window property semantics and cross-origin wrappers
insights.problem: Window properties like document/location were created with default deletable semantics and parent/top returned full Window objects across origins, exposing too much across cross-origin boundaries.
insights.root_cause: Initial property descriptor defaults and return of raw Window pointers instead of cross-origin wrappers were not aligned with web security expectations.
insights.solution: Mark core Window accessors as non-deletable and return limited CrossOriginWindow wrappers for cross-origin accesses so that property exposure and available APIs are constrained appropriately.

insights.title: DOMException code/name mapping affects WebCrypto and other APIs
insights.problem: Some internal errors (e.g., InvalidAccessError) were not mapped to DOMException types/codes, causing spec-driven behavior and tests to fail or lead to crashes.
insights.root_cause: Internal error enums and legacy DOMException code/name mapping were out of sync.
insights.solution: Map internal error types to DOMException names and legacy numeric codes consistently so WebAPI consumers (SubtleCrypto, fetch/XHR) receive expected exception types and messages.

insights.title: Timeout/Abort lifecycle must be enforced at native client and event layer
insights.problem: XMLHttpRequest lacked end-to-end timeout support; per-request timeouts couldn't be enforced and timeout events were not emitted, and abort signals were sometimes not checked during streaming callbacks.
insights.root_cause: Missing plumbing between XHR timeout property, the native libcurl/client timeout enforcement, and event dispatch semantics; similarly AbortSignal integration was incomplete during incremental callbacks.
insights.solution: Enforce timeouts at the native client boundary, check AbortSignal during headers/body callbacks, and dispatch timeout/abort events per the XHR/fetch semantics.

insights.title: Live ranges and iterators must update on character-data and node mutations
insights.problem: Ranges, NodeIterator/TreeWalker state could become stale after character-data replace/split or node insertion/removal if update hooks were incomplete.
insights.root_cause: Mutation hooks were implemented in scattered places; numeric refcount models previously tied to deinit made consistent updates harder.
insights.solution: Centralize mutation update calls (updateForCharacterDataReplace, updateForSplitText, updateForNodeInsertion/removal) and ensure live ranges/iterators receive updates in all mutation code paths while using RC lifetimes.

key_files.why: Core DOM Document implementation: node creation, id cache, import/adopt semantics and tree traversal factories; changes here affect nearly all DOM operations and identity semantics.
Global Window façade: timer scheduling, event dispatch, cross-origin wrappers and many JS-global bindings; edits change scheduler interaction and cross-origin exposure.
Fetch bridge between JS Promise and native HttpClient/Response; it must align with cookie_origin, header iteration and AbortSignal behavior used across network APIs.
XMLHttpRequest lifecycle and event semantics (progress/timeout/abort) and buffering; it coordinates native client timeouts and must maintain correct refcounting for active requests.
Visibility-observer implementation that registers with Page and delivers entries to V8; illustrates RC migration pitfalls and ordering requirements when unregistering and releasing refs.

construct_relationships: Document creates and owns Node/Element/Attribute/CData instances and keeps an elements-by-id cache; node import/adopt must update ownerDocument links and invalidate/repopulate id cache lazily.

Window exposes globals and schedules JS callbacks on the Page scheduler (setTimeout/setInterval/requestAnimationFrame), and dispatches events into the EventTarget system implemented in the event subfolder.

Fetch and XMLHttpRequest translate JS-level requests into HttpClient calls (network/http.zig) and create/resolve JS Promises or dispatch XHR events; both integrate with AbortSignal and must propagate page.url as cookie_origin (storage cookie selection).

AbortController produces AbortSignal instances used by Fetch/XHR and timers; AbortSignal integrates with the Page scheduler to implement timeout-based aborts and throwIfAborted translates aborted state into JS exceptions.

Blob objects are used by Fetch/XHR (blob: URL handling) and provide arrayBuffer/stream/text bridges to JS via JsApi; Blob lifetimes are managed by page arenas and RC semantics for cross-language exposure.

CustomElementRegistry registers definitions and upgrades elements created by Document/Parser; upgrades invoke lifecycle callbacks on Elements and interact with element/collections code to ensure connected state and mutation notifications.

AbstractRange and CData update in response to character-data edits and node insertions/removals; these updates are necessary for correct range boundaries and for live collections (collections subtree).

IntersectionObserver registers with the Page to observe element intersection state and delivers IntersectionObserverEntry objects into JS via V8-local scopes; must manage per-target previous state to detect threshold crosses.
notable_relationships: WebAPI networking code depends on the network/http.zig HttpClient and the storage cookie jar (src/browser/webapi/storage) for cookie_origin selection and header serialization — changes to headers/Response shape ripple into Fetch/XHR and higher-level consumers.

JsApi bindings across webapi types must match the V8 exposure expectations in the engine bridge and interact correctly with snapshot/V8 startup; lifetime mismatches (finalizer vs RC) can cause use-after-free across Zig/V8 boundaries.

Per-page arena allocation and page-level lp.RC reference counting are cross-cutting: many webapi modules allocate short-lived objects on the page arena but must use RC for long-lived objects handed to JS or stored across async callbacks.
DOM parsing relies on the parser bridge (html5ever) and DOMImplementation; parser errors or Document creation behavior affect CustomElementRegistry upgrades and initial element lifecycle events.

Event construction and dispatch (src/browser/webapi/event) are depended on by many modules (XHR progress events, AbortSignal abort events, Console.trace) so any change to event semantics or JsApi shapes will have broad impact.
recommendations: Preserve page arena usage patterns: short-lived temporary data should remain on the page arena; long-lived objects exposed to JS must use lp.RC acquire/release semantics.

Maintain correct acquire/unregister/release ordering: unregister listeners/observers before calling releaseRef to avoid use-after-free paths (especially for IntersectionObserver, ranges, and custom element registrations).

When editing network code, ensure cookie_origin is passed from the Page/document context and use the unified Response/header iterator callbacks; run network-related tests and validate SameSite cookie behaviors.
Ensure XHR timeouts and AbortSignal checks are enforced at native callback points (headers/body callbacks) and that timeout/abort events are dispatched per spec.

Respect Window property descriptors and cross-origin wrapper semantics: core accessors (document/location) should remain non-deletable and cross-origin accesses must return limited wrappers rather than raw Window pointers.

Run unit tests and format checks (zig build test, zig build fmt) after changes; because many behaviors cross files, run integration/end-to-end tests that exercise JS bindings, fetch/XHR flows, custom elements upgrades and event dispatch where applicable.
Be cautious modifying JsApi registration shapes or V8-exposed prototypes: mismatches can break snapshot creation, startup-time bindings, or runtime method lookups in page scripts.

src/network/cache.description: This folder implements the network response caching abstraction and a filesystem-backed cache implementation used by the networking layer. It centralizes conservative RFC9111-like cacheability policy, typed cached-response/metadata structures, durable on-disk storage (JSON metadata + body), and concurrency/atomicity guarantees so the HTTP loader can persist and reuse responses safely and evolvably.
key_behaviors: Decide whether an HTTP response is cacheable using conservative Cache-Control parsing and produce per-entry CachedMetadata.
Store cache entries durably by writing response body + versioned JSON metadata atomically to disk, keyed by SHA-256 hex.

Retrieve cache entries by key: validate metadata (expiry/age using the request timestamp), match Vary header values against the incoming request, and return file-backed CachedResponse objects for zero-copy reads.
Dispatch cache operations through the Cache union abstraction so concrete implementations (FsCache) are swappable while exposing a small API surface (deinit/get/put).
Protect concurrent on-disk operations with striped locks to prevent races and corruption during parallel get/put operations.

insights.title: Centralized metadata formatting for logs
insights.problem: Cache metadata logging was ad-hoc and verbose across callers, making debugging inconsistent.
insights.root_cause: No single helper to render key metadata fields for human consumption.
insights.solution: CachedMetadata.format() was added so callers can consistently format important fields when logging cache interactions.

insights.title: Cache.get/put signatures and request context
insights.problem: Callers previously passed simple string keys and lacked request-scoped context (timestamps/headers), leading to incorrect expiry checks and Vary matching.
insights.root_cause: Cache operations did not accept a structured request context.
insights.solution: get/put now accept a CacheRequest (including timestamp and headers) and use an arena for temporary allocations so expiry and Vary semantics can be enforced deterministically.

insights.title: Metadata serialization needs versioning
insights.problem: Ad-hoc binary serialization was brittle and hard to evolve; older cache entries could be incompatible with new code.
insights.root_cause: Lack of a stable, self-describing on-disk format for metadata.
insights.solution: FsCache switched to JSON metadata with an explicit CACHE_VERSION field to enable safe evolution and easier debugging/migration.

insights.title: Atomic writes and striped locks prevent corruption
insights.problem: Parallel file operations could corrupt cache entries or partially write data visible to readers.
insights.root_cause: Concurrent get/put operations on the same filesystem paths without fine-grained coordination.
insights.solution: Writes performed via .tmp files and rename (atomic), and striped locks were introduced to serialize conflicting operations on keys.

insights.title: Expiry and Vary must be validated against request context
insights.problem: Expired entries were being served because get used wall-clock time incorrectly or ignored stored timestamps; Vary mismatches could incorrectly return responses for different request headers.
insights.root_cause: Expiry checks did not use the stored metadata timestamp or the request timestamp; Vary matching logic was not centralized.
insights.solution: FsCache enforces deterministic expiry using the CacheRequest.timestamp and performs Vary header comparisons against the incoming request headers on get.

key_files.why: Central cache abstraction and policy: exposes Cache union API, conservative Cache-Control parsing, and shared typed representations (CachedMetadata/CachedResponse) used by callers and implementations.
Concrete durable cache implementation used in production: manages hashing, atomic file writes, JSON metadata versioning, expiry/Vary enforcement, and striped locking for concurrency safety.

construct_relationships: Cache (Cache.zig) is a discriminated union that routes deinit/get/put calls to the concrete implementation (FsCache).
CacheControl.parse (Cache.zig) is used to conservatively decide cacheability before put operations and influences CachedMetadata creation.
CachedMetadata produced by Cache flows into FsCache.put where it is serialized to JSON, and FsCache.get deserializes JSON back into CachedMetadata for expiry and Vary checks.
FsCache.hashKey produces a SHA-256 hex key used to compute on-disk paths (cachePath/cacheTmpPath); get/put use getLockPtr (striped locks) for the same key to synchronize operations.
FsCache.get returns a file-backed CachedResponse so callers can read body bytes without copying; callers must therefore handle file descriptors/streams and lifetimes indicated by CachedResponse.
notable_relationships: Upstream network/http code calls Cache.get/put and must supply a CacheRequest (timestamp + request headers); any change here requires updating those callers.

On-disk cache layout and metadata versioning are consumed outside this folder (tests, tooling, or future migration code), so changes to CACHE_VERSION or JSON schema affect compatibility with existing cache entries.

Concurrency expectations: other parts of the process perform parallel network operations that will call into the cache concurrently, so striped locks and atomic rename semantics are relied upon to prevent corruption.

Filesystem permissions and paths: FsCache builds file paths from hashed keys and expects underlying filesystem semantics (atomic rename); portability or container filesystems (e.g., Docker volumes) can affect behavior.
Logging and debugging consumers expect CachedMetadata.format() to produce consistent, readable metadata; changing that representation impacts observability.
recommendations: When editing public cache APIs (Cache.get/put/deinit), update all callers to provide the new CacheRequest context and arena usage; mismatches will silently break expiry and Vary semantics.
Preserve atomic write pattern (.tmp -> rename) and striped-lock acquisition order; changes that remove these will risk partial writes and races under concurrent operations.
Maintain JSON metadata versioning (CACHE_VERSION) and ensure deserialization remains backward-compatible or include migration logic before changing the schema.
Any change to key hashing (hashKey) or on-disk path layout must consider existing cache files and collision checks; ensure tests validate collision detection and path derivation.

Run unit and integration tests that cover: metadata serialization/deserialization, expiry logic using CacheRequest.timestamp, Vary header matching, concurrent get/put scenarios, and reading file-backed CachedResponse lifetimes.

src/browser/css.description: This folder implements the CSS tokenizer and parser used by the browser runtime to turn raw CSS text (stylesheets, style attributes) into token streams, declarations, and stylesheet rules. It is the single source of CSS token semantics and grammar-level parsing (CSS Syntax Module Level 3) and therefore is critical for correct style extraction, selector/block pairing, and downstream style application in pages.
key_behaviors: Streaming tokenization of UTF-8 CSS input into a typed Token union with precise handling for identifiers, numbers, strings, escapes, unicode-ranges, comments and delimiters.

Parsing token streams into iterators: declaration lists (properties, values, !important) and stylesheet rules (selectors paired with blocks), with logic to skip or recover from at-rules and nested blocks.

Providing low-level primitives (consumeString, consumeName, consumeComment, nextByte, hasNewlineAt) that encode tokenizer state and are relied on by parser consumers for correct syntax interpretation.
Error recovery and skipping behavior for malformed constructs (bad-strings, unexpected EOF, nested blocks) so parsing can continue and callers receive a sane iterator-based view.

insights.title: Tokenizer must preserve CSS Syntax Module Level 3 semantics
insights.problem: Deviations in tokenization (identifier escaping, unicode-ranges, string handling) lead to parser misinterpretation and incorrect declaration values or selectors.
insights.root_cause: The parser assumes the tokenizer implements CSS Syntax Level 3 token rules exactly; subtle differences (escape sequences, newline handling inside strings) break higher-level parsing.
insights.solution: Any edits must preserve the tokenizer's spec-driven behavior; if tokenizer changes affect produced Token variants or semantics, the parser and all callers must be validated and adjusted accordingly, and test vectors should cover edge cases (escapes, unicode ranges, bad-strings).

insights.title: Tight coupling between Token union and parser expectations
insights.problem: Parser code uses token discriminants and stream primitives directly; adding/removing token variants or changing token payload shapes will break parsing logic.
insights.root_cause: TokenStream and Token variants form the API surface between Tokenizer.zig and Parser.zig; there is no intermediate adapter to absorb breaking changes.
insights.solution: Maintain API/ABI stability of Token and TokenStream behavior, or ensure coordinated updates across both files and all callers; when adjusting tokens, update parser consumption patterns and the iterator implementations simultaneously.

insights.title: Correctness depends on precise UTF-8 decoding and escape semantics
insights.problem: Incorrect byte-level handling (nextByte, newline detection, escape sequences) causes mis-tokenization of identifiers/strings and corrupts values exposed to higher layers.
insights.root_cause: Tokenizer implements low-level byte consumption and escape resolution directly; mistakes here propagate into parsed values.
insights.solution: Preserve the low-level helpers' semantics and invariants; any change must respect UTF-8 boundaries, newline detection rules, and escape/unicode-range resolution to avoid changing parsed text content.

insights.title: Iterators expose streamed parse results and depend on stable memory/alloc semantics
insights.problem: Changing return-by-reference patterns, allocator use, or lifetime management in iterators can introduce use-after-free, copying, or allocation regressions.
insights.root_cause: DeclarationsIterator/RulesIterator and TokenStream are designed for streaming parsing with specific lifetime assumptions in Zig; callers expect stable references until the iterator advances.
insights.solution: Keep iterator prototypes, ownership expectations, and allocator interactions consistent; when altering memory behavior, ensure all call sites (and tests) reflect the new lifetimes and that allocations are bounded for streaming scenarios.

insights.title: Parser must gracefully skip malformed constructs and at-rules
insights.problem: Incomplete handling of at-rules or nested blocks can halt parsing or return incomplete rule lists, affecting stylesheet application.
insights.root_cause: Parser implements skip/consume logic for at-rules and nested blocks; changes to skipping behavior or block recognition change which rules are emitted.
insights.solution: Preserve or explicitly coordinate any change to skipping/recovery semantics and ensure downstream consumers receive the expected set of rules/declarations; expand tests to cover malformed inputs and at-rule edge cases when modifications are made.

key_files.why: Implements the low-level CSS Syntax Level 3 tokenization; produces the Token union and stream primitives that the parser consumes.
Consumes Tokenizer tokens to produce DeclarationsIterator and RulesIterator; extracts properties, values, !important and selector/block pairs and implements recovery for at-rules.

construct_relationships: Tokenizer.Token (Token union) defines the token shapes (ident, string, number, delim, bad-string, EOF, etc.) that Parser.TokenStream reads and interprets.

Tokenizer helpers (consumeString, consumeName, consumeComment, nextByte, hasNewlineAt) provide the low-level operations that Parser uses implicitly via TokenStream behavior to assemble declaration values and selectors.
Parser.DeclarationsIterator and Parser.RulesIterator iterate over tokens produced by Tokenizer.advance and rely on TokenStream invariants for peeking, consuming, and skipping blocks.
Parser's at-rule skipping and nested block consumption logic depends on correct delimiting tokens (e.g., '{', '}', ';', EOF) produced by the tokenizer.
notable_relationships: The parser/tokenizer output is consumed by higher-level browser components (Page, Browser runtime, style application) to apply styles and expose computed or dumped DOM styling — changes here affect rendering and CSS-based behaviors.
Network and HTML parsing components supply the raw stylesheet or inline style text that feed into this tokenizer/parser; encoding assumptions (UTF-8) and input trimming must align across modules.
Because the tokenizer and parser form a small, spec-bound subsystem, any changes ripple to tests, tooling, and possibly CDP behaviors that surface styles or style-related dumps.
recommendations: Do not change Token union discriminants, payload shapes, or TokenStream peek/advance semantics without simultaneously updating Parser.zig and all callers that consume tokens.
Respect existing UTF-8, newline, escape and unicode-range handling invariants; validate edits against edge-case inputs (escaped identifiers, bad-strings, EOF-in-string).
Preserve iterator lifetimes and ownership expectations: ensure DeclarationsIterator/RulesIterator semantics (what is valid between advances) remain stable or that all callers are updated.

When modifying skip/recovery behavior for at-rules or malformed blocks, run the repository's unit tests and any CSS edge-case test suites to ensure upstream consumers still receive expected rule sets.

Run formatting and the project's build/tests (zig build fmt, zig build test / make test) after changes; coordinate changes with callers in the browser/page/style layers since behavior here directly affects runtime output.

src/browser/parser.description: This folder implements the bridge between the embedded html5ever HTML/XML parser (Rust shim) and the browser runtime's internal DOM model. It exposes FFI types and functions that the Rust side calls into and provides Parser entry points (document, fragment, streaming) which translate html5ever callbacks into creation/attachment of Page/Node/Element structures. It is critical because it is the only place that glues external parsing logic to in-process DOM state and must preserve ABI, ownership and lifecycle invariants.
key_behaviors: Entrypoints to parse full documents, fragments, and incremental/streaming input, producing the runtime's DOM nodes.
FFI surface declaration of html5ever parser functions, iterator types, NodeOrText/QualName shapes and memory-usage reporting used across the bridge.
Callback-driven DOM construction: the html5ever shim invokes createElement/append/pop and related callbacks to mutate Page/Node/Element structures.
Streaming lifecycle management: create -> feed (multiple) -> finish -> destroy, with Parser responsible for matching those calls and preserving parser-local state.
Attribute and node iteration via iterator helpers exposed by the FFI; memory usage introspection via html5ever_get_memory_usage for telemetry/monitoring.

insights.title: Tight ABI contract between Zig callbacks and the html5ever Rust shim
insights.problem: The html5ever shim expects exact C-compatible types and callback signatures; any mismatch in struct layout, field order, or calling convention will cause memory corruption or crashes.
insights.root_cause: The parser bridge declares C externs and helper types (Slice, QualName, NodeOrText, Nullable) that must mirror the Rust side exactly; there is no automatic binding generation or safety barrier.
insights.solution: Treat the Zig declarations as a strict contract: keep extern signatures, struct fields, and enums identical to the Rust shim, and update both sides simultaneously. When editing these files, verify Cargo/build produces the expected symbols and run integration tests that exercise parsing codepaths.

insights.title: Callbacks cross language/runtime boundaries with shared ownership assumptions
insights.problem: Callbacks invoked by html5ever create/attach nodes into Page/Node structures; incorrect handling of references, temporaries, or allocator use can leak memory or cause use-after-free.
insights.root_cause: Parser implements immediate DOM mutation in response to external parser callbacks and must correctly convert transient FFI pointers (e.g., slices, NodeOrText) into owned runtime objects with correct lifetimes.
insights.solution: Maintain explicit ownership rules: convert FFI-provided data into owned Zig allocations before storing on Page/Node structures, and ensure parser streaming finalize/cleanup (destroy) paths free parser-local state. When changing node representations or callback logic, validate with memory-leak tests and streaming termination scenarios.

insights.title: Parser callbacks may be invoked in streaming/async contexts requiring careful reentrancy handling
insights.problem: Streaming parsing and feed/finish operations can be invoked while other browser components interact with the same Page/DOM; race conditions or reentrant access could corrupt DOM state.
insights.root_cause: The parser bridge is single-threaded in design but the runtime may schedule network streaming, script execution, and CDP events concurrently; callbacks are invoked from the Rust shim's execution context.
insights.solution: Assume callbacks occur in the parser's expected execution context and preserve any mutex/guarding conventions used by the surrounding runtime. When editing Parser.zig, do not introduce blocking operations or assumptions about thread locality; ensure streaming create/feed/finish/destroy are always paired and that DOM mutation invariants remain intact.

insights.title: Streaming parser state must be created, fed, finished and destroyed in order
insights.problem: Mismatch between create/feed/finish/destroy calls or failure to handle partial input can leave the parser in an inconsistent state or leak resources.
insights.root_cause: html5ever offers incremental parse APIs; the Parser implementation must map those to the runtime lifetime exactly and handle mid-stream errors or early termination.
insights.solution: Preserve the lifecycle mapping and ensure all code paths (successful finish, early abort, error) invoke the corresponding destroy/cleanup. Tests that feed partial input, then finish or abort, should be run after any change to parsing code.

insights.title: Memory usage and attribute iteration helpers are relied on by higher layers
insights.problem: html5ever_get_memory_usage and attribute iterator semantics are used for telemetry and DOM inspection; breaking or changing these helpers could skew telemetry and break consumers.
insights.root_cause: The parser exposes a small API surface beyond pure parsing (memory introspection, attribute iteration) that other runtime components depend on.
insights.solution: Keep the memory-usage API and iterator semantics stable. If the representation of attributes or memory counters changes, update telemetry consumers and validate reported values with integration tests.

key_files.why: Implements the translation layer from html5ever callbacks to the runtime DOM; contains parse entrypoints, streaming management and all callback handlers that create/attach nodes.
Declares the C ABI surface and low-level types used by the parser bridge; changes here alter the contract with the Rust html5ever shim.

construct_relationships: Parser.zig calls into and is driven by the extern functions declared in html5ever.zig (parse_document, streaming parser create/feed/finish/destroy).
html5ever callbacks map NodeOrText/QualName/AttributeIterator items to internal ParsedNode/Element constructions in Parser.zig.
Streaming struct/state in Parser.zig mirrors the lifecycle exposed by the html5ever streaming parser functions; feed/finish/destroy transitions must be honored.
html5ever_get_memory_usage exposed in html5ever.zig is consulted by higher-level telemetry/monitoring code via Parser.zig or other callers for memory reporting.
notable_relationships: Upstream: The Rust/html5ever shim (separate crate) must remain in sync with the Zig FFI types; any change to either side requires coordinated updates and a full rebuild (Cargo + Zig build).
Downstream: Page/Node/Element definitions in src/browser are mutated by Parser callbacks — changes to the DOM shapes elsewhere require corresponding changes in Parser.zig conversion/attachment logic.
Build: The build pipeline involves both zig build and cargo build. Editing FFI signatures or adding new externs will require rebuilding the Rust shim and ensuring build.zig invokes cargo correctly.

Telemetry & tests: Memory usage and streaming parser flows are exercised by higher-level telemetry, unit and end-to-end tests; breaking ABI, lifecycle or iterator semantics will surface in those tests and CI.
recommendations: Do not change C extern signatures, struct field order, or types in html5ever.zig without coordinating updates to the Rust shim; verify symbol names and sizes remain identical.

When modifying Parser.zig, preserve ownership rules: convert FFI-provided buffers into owned runtime allocations before retaining them, and ensure all destroy/cleanup paths are invoked for streaming parsers.
