Respect streaming lifecycle: any edits must keep create -> feed* -> finish -> destroy ordering and handle early aborts; add/retain tests that feed partial streams and abort mid-stream.
Assume callbacks can be executed in the parser/shim execution context — avoid introducing blocking or thread-unsafe operations in callbacks and validate with concurrency/reentrancy tests.

After changes, run a full build that includes cargo (html5ever crate) and zig build; run unit tests exercising parsing, streaming, attribute iteration, and memory-usage telemetry, plus relevant end-to-end navigation tests.

src/browser.description: The src/browser folder implements the core in-process browser runtime: page/frame lifecycle, DOM and WebAPI surfaces, script/module loading and V8 integration, and the HTTP client/interaction layers used by pages and protocol handlers. It centralizes lifecycle, memory/arena ownership, reference-counting and cross-cutting semantics (networking, events, visibility, input simulation) that must remain consistent for correctness and memory safety across the runtime and CDP/MCP surfaces.
key_behaviors: Page lifecycle management: create/destroy pages and frames, initialize per-page arenas and V8 contexts, orchestrate navigation, parse/load state, and deterministic cleanup.
Network request flow: queueing requests, robots.txt handling, caching, interception, aborts and response delivery through a Response abstraction to Fetch/XHR/ScriptManager/Page.
Script/module loading and execution: schedule/resolve/evaluate inline, external, async/defer/ES module scripts while preventing V8 reentrancy and guaranteeing ordering.
Event/input & action emulation: synthesize DOM events (click/fill/hover/press/select/scroll), run macrotasks/microtasks, wait helpers and notify CDP clients of state changes.
V8 isolate & context coordination: manage Env/Context lifetimes, finalizers, global/temporary handle storage, and bridge native Zig types into JS with identity and origin scoping.

insights.title: Centralize Response semantics and avoid Transfer pointer assumptions
insights.problem: Multiple modules (Page callbacks, ScriptManager, Fetch, XHR) relied on Transfer-specific pointers or old curl transfer-private assumptions that changed after refactors, causing crashes or incorrect metadata access.
insights.root_cause: Network layer refactor moved transport into Connection.transport and introduced a Response wrapper, but many higher-level consumers still expect Transfer internals or private pointers.
insights.solution: Treat HttpClient.Response as the canonical callback payload; avoid casting from Connection/Transfer internals, use header iterators, and ensure client-level configs (e.g., max_response_size) are enforced consistently.

insights.title: Client-level transfer limits and robots/caching coordination
insights.problem: Max-response-size and robots-fetch sequencing were inconsistently enforced across request paths and caches causing unexpected large payloads or stalled requests waiting for robots.txt.
insights.root_cause: Some limits lived on Transfer while request queuing, robots fetching and cache serving logically belong to a shared Client tied to browser/session lifecycle.
insights.solution: Enforce max_response_size and robots fetch semantics centrally in HttpClient.Client so all consumers (scripts, XHR, fetch, script preloads) observe uniform behavior.

insights.title: Session-level finalizers and identity management
insights.problem: Finalizers, TAOs and temporary/global handle tracking scattered across contexts caused double-finalize, use-after-free, and lifetime ordering bugs during teardown.
insights.root_cause: Per-context allocation and finalizer semantics did not match cross-context/shared-origin lifetimes; prior mixture of weak/strong booleans and implicit fallbacks created fragile ordering.
insights.solution: Centralize globals/temps/finalizer callbacks at Session scope, move TAO allocation out of context-level arenas, and migrate finalizer model to explicit reference-counting (acquire/release) to avoid double-finalize and improper freeing.

insights.title: Context/isolate mapping and handle lifetimes
insights.problem: Context-level storage of identity/TAO could be freed while other contexts in the same isolate still referenced it, breaking module and global handles.
insights.root_cause: TAOs and identity structures were allocated in a context arena; contexts were destroyed independently while TAOs must outlive contexts for origin-sharing.
insights.solution: Allocate TAOs and identity-temporary storage in session/isolate-level arenas and ensure Env/Context create/destroy semantics map Zig Contexts to v8::Context cleanly and pump micro/macrotasks appropriately.

insights.title: Synchronous-cache evaluation can re-enter ScriptManager
insights.problem: When cached scripts are returned synchronously, evaluate() could run while addFromElement was still mutating ScriptManager state, causing inconsistent guards (is_evaluating) and ordering violations.
insights.root_cause: Synchronous callbacks from cache bypassed the asynchronous setup steps and guard toggles expected by the addFromElement flow.
insights.solution: Protect synchronous-cache paths by scheduling evaluation after element setup completes or by guarding reentrancy explicitly so state mutations and is_evaluating flags remain consistent.

insights.title: Reference-counting and event dispatch ordering must match browser semantics
insights.problem: Event dispatch code released references that were not acquired or used wrong order for input/change and click sequences, leading to leaks or missing event-side-effects.
insights.root_cause: Event handling and action helpers duplicated logic and assumed element states (focus, active element) that sometimes were absent; ordering of dispatch vs state change differed from browser expectations.
insights.solution: Ensure event acquisitions/releases are balanced (do not release un-acquired refs), focus targets prior to fill, and preserve browser ordering for setChecked/click/input/change event sequences.

key_files.why: Central HTTP client used by Page, Fetch, XHR and ScriptManager; enforces robots, cache, queuing, interception, aborts and response-size limits.
Core browsing-context owner: DOM, JS context integration, navigation orchestration, and lifecycle/resource cleanup for frames and root pages.
Encapsulates script loading/execution semantics and coordinates with HttpClient and V8; sensitive to reentrancy and evaluation ordering.
Manages session-level lifetimes, origin sharing, V8 finalizer semantics and deterministic cleanup across pages/contexts.
Creates/manages the V8 Isolate and pumps micro/macrotasks; its allocation and callback wiring determine memory and execution guarantees.

construct_relationships: HttpClient.Client is consumed by Page, Fetch, XMLHttpRequest and ScriptManager to schedule and complete all network operations; it must present a consistent Response ABI.
Page owns the DOM and per-page arenas and coordinates with ScriptManager for script scheduling and with Runner for ticking and wait logic.
ScriptManager issues requests through HttpClient and calls into V8 via Context/Env to evaluate scripts; it must avoid reentrancy into its own setup when cache returns synchronously.
Session aggregates Pages, tracks origins and finalizers, and provides allocate/cleanup semantics used by Context (V8) and Window; finalizers and TAOs are expected to be session-scoped.
Env creates Isolate-level templates and manages Context instances; Bridge (js/bridge.zig) provides the mapping of Zig-native APIs into these V8 contexts used by WebAPI modules.

StyleManager and Interactive/Forms modules rely on Page DOM state and Element helpers (Element.isDisabled) and must register nodes with the shared registry to produce backendNodeId for CDP interactions.
Runner polls HttpClient and advances Page macrotasks/microtasks (via Env/Context) to satisfy wait/until conditions used by CLI, CDP handlers and tests.
notable_relationships: This subtree depends on src/network/http.zig (libcurl bindings) and must follow its Response/header iterator and connection semantics.
Parser (src/browser/parser and src/html5ever Rust shim) produces DOM nodes that Page/Document consume; changes to DOM allocation/ownership or node registration affect parser FFI contracts.

js subfolder (Env/Context/bridge) provides the V8 embedding; changes to finalizer/refcount or handle storage here impact all JS-exposed WebAPIs in webapi/ and must preserve snapshot/init invariants used by build.zig snapshot flow.

css and StyleManager integration affect behavior of hit-testing, event targeting and DOM dumps used by actions and interactive collectors; selector bucketing and visibility assumptions are performance-sensitive.
CDP/MCP protocol handlers depend on backendNodeId and registration semantics produced by Interactive and Forms: missing backend ids break remote actions (click/fill) and tests (mcp_actions.html).
recommendations: When modifying networking paths: adopt and use HttpClient.Response and header iterator APIs everywhere; do not rely on Transfer or curl private pointers; ensure client-level max_response_size and robots/caching logic remain enforced for all request entry points.

When changing lifetimes or arenas: preserve Session-level ownership for TAOs, globals and finalizers; ensure Context deinit does not free session-scoped objects and that finalizer ordering cannot double-finalize objects.

When touching ScriptManager or script evaluation flows: protect against synchronous cache-return reentrancy (is_evaluating guard) and verify ordering guarantees for inline/async/defer/module scripts with tests.

When editing event/action code: maintain balanced acquire/release of RC refs (never release an un-acquired ref) and preserve browser-consistent ordering for focus/input/change/click sequences; run the mcp_actions.html-driven tests.

When updating V8 bridge/Env/Context: keep mapping invariants between Zig Context and v8::Context, ensure micro/macrotask pumping semantics remain intact, and confirm snapshot/template usage in builds/tests (snapshot_creator path).

Run relevant unit and integration tests after edits: zig build test (browser tests), the XHR and MCP action HTML test fixtures, and any CDP-driven end-to-end tests that exercise backendNodeId, Fetch/XHR, script loading and teardown/finalizer behavior.

src/mcp.description: This subtree implements the MCP (machine control / JSON-RPC-like) surface used by external agents to control the browser: canonical protocol types and JSON helpers, a line-oriented router that parses and dispatches incoming messages, tool and resource registries that implement callable actions and streamed page outputs, and the server harness that wires these pieces to the browser session and node registry. It is the authoritative boundary for agent communications and must maintain stable message shapes, framing semantics, error contracts, and streaming semantics because clients and tests rely on them.
key_behaviors: Frame, parse and validate newline-delimited JSON messages from an input Reader into typed protocol.Request values, enforcing a maximum message size and returning protocol-level errors on malformed input.
Map JSON-RPC methods to internal enums and dispatch requests to method-specific handlers (initialize, ping, tools, resources, notifications), using an arena for transient parsing lifetimes.

Expose a tools registry to list schemas and accept tool call requests: parse tool arguments, ensure page/session readiness (optionally navigate), execute page operations and stream or return results, mapping failures to MCP errors.

Expose a resources registry that lists available page resources and lazily serializes/streams page HTML or markdown via a StreamingText jsonStringify hook; handle serialization failures and validation of resource URIs.
Format and send JSON-RPC responses, results, and errors to clients using compact/minified serialization and escaping helpers; initialize handshake returns capability and version information.

insights.title: Unused ImageContent and version constant exposure
insights.problem: Protocol contained multiple CallToolResult content variants (including ImageContent) that are now unused and tests / code refer to a mcp.Version.latest constant inconsistently.
insights.root_cause: Tooling and screenshot functionality evolved; protocol constants for versioning were not exposed/renamed consistently across modules.
insights.solution: Treat protocol type shapes and exported version constants as authoritative API — changes affect client compatibility and tests. When modifying types or renaming version constants, update all callsites and test expectations and ensure the initialize response continues to advertise the expected version value.

insights.title: Message-size handling and framing semantics
insights.problem: Router enforces a max message size but historical behavior differed on oversized messages (previously logged and dropped without returning a protocol error).
insights.root_cause: Size limit enforcement lived inside the reader loop and did not consistently translate oversized input into a protocol-level error response.
insights.solution: Respect the newline-delimited JSON framing and ensure oversized or malformed messages produce well-formed MCP error responses where appropriate; changes to framing/limits must preserve or explicitly update observable error behavior for clients and tests.

insights.title: Protocol version configured via environment/config
insights.problem: MCP version helper moved between modules and default version was changed (affecting tests and client expectations).
insights.root_cause: Configuration responsibilities were split and default constants were adjusted without centralized propagation.
insights.solution: When editing router/initialize behavior, keep configuration of protocol version explicit and consistent with build/test expectations; any change to the default protocol version requires coordinated updates to initialize responses, tests, and external clients.

insights.title: Lazy StreamingText serialization must map errors to MCP errors
insights.problem: Resource and tool streaming previously duplicated logic and sometimes failed to report serialization errors back to the client, risking silent failures or broken JSON output.
insights.root_cause: StreamingText jsonStringify hooks perform lazy serialization; duplicated handlers did not uniformly capture and translate serialization failures into protocol-level errors.
insights.solution: Ensure StreamingText jsonStringify implementations and callers reliably capture serialization failures and return well-formed protocol errors; maintain the lazy serialization contract expected by the JSON serializer so errors surface to clients rather than producing invalid JSON.

insights.title: Node and page resolution must be consistent and deterministic
insights.problem: Multiple tool handlers duplicated node/page resolution logic and produced inconsistent error messages (e.g., specific 'Timeout waiting for page load' vs generic 'error waiting for page load').
insights.root_cause: Per-handler ad hoc checks for session.currentPage and node registry lookup led to varied error semantics.
insights.solution: When modifying tool handlers, preserve the canonical resolution patterns and error mappings: verify session.currentPage, resolve nodes via the registry, and map runner/timeout/completion states to the agreed protocol error messages so client-facing semantics remain stable.

key_files.why: Defines the authoritative JSON shapes, serialization/deserialization helpers, escaping and embedded-schema minification used across the MCP surface; changes here are breaking for clients and tests.
Implements framing, parsing, dispatch and top-level error handling for newline-delimited MCP messages and determines routing to method handlers and version/limits enforcement.
Contains the tool registry, schemas, argument parsing and the implementations that operate on pages (navigation, DOM actions) — core functionality exposed to agents.
Server harness that composes the router, browser session and node registry and provides sendResponse/sendError helpers used by all MCP handlers; integration tests target this file.
Provides resource listing and lazy streaming of page HTML/markdown via StreamingText jsonStringify; serialization behavior here impacts resource reads and error reporting.

construct_relationships: router.processRequests reads newline-delimited JSON from a Reader and parses into protocol.Request using an arena allocated for the request lifetime.

router.handleMessage maps the request.method string to a Method enum and dispatches to handlers that live in tools.zig and resources.zig, returning protocol.Response or protocol.Error back via Server.sendResponse/sendError.
Server holds the Browser/session and node_registry references; tool handlers call Server.browser/session APIs (ensurePage, performGoto) and node_registry lookups to resolve nodes referenced by tools.

protocol.jsonStringify and JsonEscapingWriter are used when serializing Response/results; StreamingText.jsonStringify in resources.zig is invoked lazily during that serialization to produce large HTML/markdown payloads.

tools.zig may perform navigation via network/http and build DOM/state that other components (e.g., resources streaming) rely on — tool execution mutates session/page state visible to subsequent MCP calls.
notable_relationships: This MCP layer depends on browser runtime modules (src/browser/*) for session/currentPage, navigation, and node registry; changes to MCP request handling or tool semantics may require coordinated expectations from browser and page APIs.
protocol versioning and default value flows are referenced by tests and external clients; the default protocol version is also influenced by global config/environment code outside the mcp subtree.

StreamingText serialization touches the HTML/markdown generation pipeline which crosses into the html5ever Rust bridge and DOM serialization code in the browser layer; serialization failures may originate outside mcp and must be mapped appropriately.

Router’s framing and size limits interact with I/O code and any caller that provides raw input (CLI, stdin, websockets); changing framing semantics impacts upstream callers and tests that simulate framed input.
recommendations: Do not change public JSON field names, enum discriminants, or the exported protocol types without updating all callsites and unit/integration tests that assert message shapes (run zig build test).

Preserve newline-delimited JSON framing and message-size enforcement semantics; if behavior is intentionally changed, update router behavior and tests and ensure clients receive appropriate MCP error responses for oversized/malformed messages.

When touching version constants or initialize result contents, ensure the value propagated to initialize responses matches test and client expectations (the default version is relied on by tests and external clients).

When editing tool schemas or tool_list/tool_map entries, update both the schema listings and the runtime parsing/validation code so clients see consistent schema advertising and runtime argument handling.
Maintain the arena lifetime assumptions used by the parser: parsed Request objects are transient and tied to the arena used by the router; avoid returning references that outlive that arena.
Ensure StreamingText.jsonStringify implementations remain lazy and that any serialization errors are converted into well-formed protocol errors (do not emit partial/invalid JSON).
Run formatting and unit/integration tests after edits (zig build fmt; zig build test) because CI/test suites and integration tests in Server.zig depend on exact serialized output and error messages.

src/network.description: The src/network subtree implements the browser's networking runtime: a libcurl-backed HTTP client and connection pool, a durable filesystem response cache, robots.txt rules, request signing, and low-level WebSocket handling. It matters because this layer mediates all outbound and inbound network traffic for navigation, CDP websockets and telemetry, enforces cache correctness and concurrency guarantees, and integrates libcurl into the process poll/wakeup model used throughout the binary.
key_behaviors: Manage and drive a libcurl multi handle and associated poll/wakeup loop to perform concurrent HTTP(S) transfers and integrate accepted sockets
Create, configure and reset per-connection curl easy handles (timeouts, proxy/TLS, read/write/header callbacks) and expose response/connect metadata
Provide an HTTP response caching pipeline: determine cacheability, store versioned JSON metadata + body atomically to disk, and return file-backed responses for zero-copy reads
Handle WebSocket lifecycle: upgrade, frame parsing/assembly (including fragmentation and masking), ping/pong/close semantics and server-side connection acceptance
Enforce robots.txt policy and sign outgoing requests with Web-Bot Ed25519 signatures prior to submission

insights.title: Libcurl multi integration and poll loop must coordinate with accepted sockets and websockets
insights.problem: Mixing long-lived WebSocket connections and regular HTTP transfers could starve one class of traffic and made it hard to correctly drive curl_multi alongside accepted socket I/O.
insights.root_cause: The event loop required a consolidated view of libcurl file descriptors, accepted sockets and a wakeup mechanism; previously there was insufficient separation and lifecycle control for websocket vs HTTP transports.
insights.solution: Centralize pollfd management, use a wakeup pipe to interrupt poll, maintain separate pools/limits for websocket connections, and ensure curl_multi_perform and transfer completion processing are driven from a single synchronized event loop.

insights.title: Cache expiry and metadata versioning are required to avoid serving stale or incompatible entries
insights.problem: Expired cache entries were sometimes served or metadata formats were brittle when evolving storage layout.
insights.root_cause: Callers and FsCache used inconsistent timestamps and ad-hoc metadata serialization, and Cache.get didn't reliably check entry age against the original request timestamp or stored metadata.
insights.solution: Store versioned JSON metadata, require Cache.get/put to accept a CacheRequest with timestamp and headers, and validate entry expiry and Vary matching deterministically on reads.

insights.title: Filesystem cache operations must be atomic and concurrency-safe
insights.problem: Concurrent get/put on the same cache key could lead to partial reads, races or corruption.
insights.root_cause: Parallel readers/writers operated on the same file paths without locking and used non-atomic write patterns.
insights.solution: Use atomic write pattern (write to .tmp then rename), add striped locks around per-key operations to avoid contention while preventing corruption, and verify key hash collisions.

insights.title: Connection representation must handle mixed transports and consistent header collection
insights.problem: WebSocket code and transfer code clashed over storing Transfer pointers in curl easy handles, and header data lived in multiple formats with distinct iterators.
insights.root_cause: Curl easy private was used to store specific Transfer pointers, making mixed transport handling fragile; header collection APIs were fragmented.
insights.solution: Introduce a transport union on Connection to represent Transfer vs WebSocket state, provide per-connection timeout setters (CURLOPT_TIMEOUT_MS), and expose unified header iterator/collector helpers that normalize headers from different sources.

insights.title: Tests must not assume sockets are non-blocking
insights.problem: Unit tests using socketpair failed when the test environment left sockets blocking and code asserted non-blocking flags.
insights.root_cause: Implementation asserted non-blocking socket state instead of tolerating different test harness behaviors.
insights.solution: Avoid hard assertions about non-blocking state in production APIs and make test helpers tolerant of sockets created by socketpair() without O_NONBLOCK.

key_files.why: Central runtime that owns curl multi handle, connection pools, pollfd/wakeup integration, accepts sockets and mediates concurrent network operations.
Low-level curl wrapper that creates and configures easy handles, sets callbacks, manages per-connection options and exposes response/connect metadata used by the client layer.
High-level cache policy and API (cacheability rules, CacheRequest/Metadata) that the network runtime calls into to decide get/put behavior.
Filesystem-backed cache implementation providing atomic writes, JSON metadata versioning, striped locking and file-backed CachedResponse objects for zero-copy reads.
Implements WebSocket framing, masking, fragmentation and WsConnection wrapper used by the Network runtime to manage websocket transport.

construct_relationships: Network.zig drives libcurl multi operations and uses Connection/Transfer concepts defined/managed by http.zig to create per-request easy handles and callbacks.
http.zig exposes header parsing and per-connection metadata that Cache.zig and FsCache.zig consult when deciding cacheability or storing response metadata (Vary, age, timestamps).
Cache.zig defines the CacheRequest/CachedMetadata shapes which FsCache.zig persists and validates (expiration, Vary matching) during Cache.get/put calls invoked from the network runtime.

Network.zig accepts inbound sockets and hands them to websocket.zig's WsConnection to perform upgrades, frame parsing and server-side websocket semantics; websocket transport is represented on the Connection transport union in http.zig.
WebBotAuth signs outbound requests and mutates Http.Headers prior to http.zig configuration so signatures are present when curl read/header callbacks are invoked.
notable_relationships: Browser/Page code calls into the network layer (HttpClient) which relies on http.zig and Network.zig to perform navigation requests; changes here can affect page loads and CDP-visible timing/headers.
Network now holds an App pointer for arena_pool usage and to ensure cache deinit sequencing — edits must remain compatible with the top-level App struct and its lifecycle management.

Telemetry, CDP server and other subsystems use the network APIs for reporting and websocket signaling; altering poll/wakeup behavior, wakeup pipe semantics or connection pools will have system-wide effects.

Cache implementation and its on-disk layout are relied on by long-running servers and CI artifacts; changing metadata formats or keys impacts persisted caches and may require migration or version bumping (CACHE_VERSION).
WebBotAuth depends on the project's crypto wrapper (sys/libcrypto path); changing crypto imports or signature formats can break request authentication consumers and unit tests.
recommendations: Preserve thread-safety: respect existing mutexes/striped locks and any shared access conventions when modifying pools, cache operations or poll loop logic.
Maintain atomic file-write semantics and CACHE_VERSION handling in FsCache: writes must continue to use .tmp + rename and reads must validate stored metadata timestamps and Vary values.
Do not break curl integration semantics: keep the wakeup pipe + pollfd coordination, proper ordering of curl_multi_perform and transfer-completion processing, and per-connection timeout application.
Keep the Connection transport abstraction stable (or update all call sites): if you change how curl easy private or transport union is used, update websocket and transfer handling consistently.

Run the network unit tests and full test suite (zig build test / make test) after changes; run end-to-end navigation and websocket scenarios where possible because small changes can affect timing and poll interactions.
When changing APIs that require arena_pool or App lifetime, update callers in higher-level modules (Browser, main) to pass the App pointer and ensure cache deinit happens before App teardown.
Avoid assumptions about socket blocking mode in websocket code and tests; make behavior tolerant to blocking sockets or ensure test harness sets non-blocking mode explicitly.

src/cdp.description: This directory implements the Chrome DevTools Protocol (CDP) surface for Lightpanda: a JSON->domain router, per-domain command handlers, node/AX serialization, and a realistic test harness that simulates websocket framing. It is the protocol bridge between external DevTools-compatible clients (Puppeteer/Playwright/etc.) and the internal browser runtime (BrowserContext, Page, Network, Cookie.Jar, Inspector). Correctness here is critical for client compatibility (event ordering, payload shapes, session mapping) and for network/interception behaviors exposed to clients.
key_behaviors: Receive, parse and validate incoming CDP JSON messages and map them to domain-specific handlers.
Maintain BrowserContext lifecycle and map CDP sessionId/target ids to internal Session/Page and emit CDP events back to clients.
Translate internal runtime events/state (network transfers, navigation lifecycle, DOM mutations, dialogs) into ordered CDP events and domain responses.
Provide node registry, search lifecycle, and serialize DOM/AX nodes into CDP-compatible JSON structures for remote clients.
Support request interception (Fetch), cookie/Storage operations, and basic emulation/security stubs to appear DevTools-compatible.
Exercise the real websocket framing semantics in unit tests via a socketpair-based harness that drives the production Server.Client.

insights.title: Move from anytype/generics to concrete CDP.Command types
insights.problem: Domain handlers previously used comptime generics or anytype for Command and BrowserContext which increased call-site complexity and allowed inconsistent handler signatures.
insights.root_cause: Use of Zig generics/anytype at domain boundaries made it unclear which concrete types domains expect and forced repeated comptime plumbing.
insights.solution: Domain APIs were changed to accept concrete *CDP.Command and concrete BrowserContext types; maintain consistent signatures across domains so callers and tests use the same concrete types.

insights.title: Page lifecycle event ordering must match DevTools expectations
insights.problem: Page events (frameNavigated, domContentEventFired, loadEventFired, lifecycle events) were emitted in an order different from Chrome, causing client-visible behavioral differences.
insights.root_cause: Navigation lifecycle hooks and event emission were coupled with document load timing and emitted too late or out of sequence.
insights.solution: Emit Page.* events in the order DevTools clients expect; preserve ordering assumptions when changing navigation or lifecycle code paths to avoid breaking downstream clients.

insights.title: Include Cookie header when serializing Transfers for Network.requestWillBeSent
insights.problem: Serialized request objects emitted to CDP clients omitted the Cookie header, producing incomplete request payloads for clients inspecting network traffic.
insights.root_cause: Transfer-to-CDP serialization omitted cookies when building the request payload for CDP events.
insights.solution: Ensure Transfer serialization includes the Cookie header and any client-set extra headers so emitted Network.* events reflect the true request headers.

insights.title: Proxy restoration API and BrowserContext teardown must match network refactors
insights.problem: BrowserContext cleanup called restoreOriginalProxy which may no longer exist or be the correct call after proxy refactors.
insights.root_cause: Network/proxy APIs changed (e.g., changeProxy semantics) but BrowserContext teardown retained the old restore API call.
insights.solution: BrowserContext cleanup should invoke the canonical proxy reset API (e.g., changeProxy(null)) and be kept aligned with http/network layer changes to avoid leaking proxy state.

insights.title: Centralize storage of scripts that run on every new document
insights.problem: There was no centralized BrowserContext storage for scripts registered with addScriptToEvaluateOnNewDocument, causing inconsistent behavior across pages or missing script injection.
insights.root_cause: Scripts were tracked ad-hoc per-page or via disparate locations instead of a single BrowserContext list.
insights.solution: Store scripts_on_new_document centrally on the BrowserContext and ensure Page navigation/mutation paths consult that list when creating new docs/isolated worlds.

insights.title: CDP tests must exercise real websocket framing and allow event-loop ticks
insights.problem: Unit tests that compared serialized JSON values were brittle and the fake client did not simulate websocket framing partial reads or timing, leading to flaky tests.
insights.root_cause: Previous test harness used an ad-hoc fake Client; comparisons were non-deterministic and tests could hang if page ticks weren’t advanced.
insights.solution: Use a socketpair-backed Server.Client and parse WebSocket frames; tests should advance the page runner/event loop and compare fully serialized JSON frames for deterministic results.

insights.title: AX serialization must respect element disability semantics like fieldset-disabled
insights.problem: AX writer previously relied on naive attribute checks and missed semantics such as fieldset-induced disabled state.
insights.root_cause: AX generation used hasAttribute checks rather than canonical Element.isDisabled semantics.
insights.solution: Use Element.isDisabled or equivalent canonical checks when computing accessibility properties to match browser behavior and client expectations.

insights.title: Domain handlers must use canonical http types
insights.problem: Some domains used legacy/net_http types while core networking moved to http.Header and http.Method, causing mismatched imports/renames.
insights.root_cause: Refactors moved or renamed networking types but domain code was not updated everywhere.
insights.solution: Ensure domains import and use the unified http types (http.Header, http.Method) so signatures and runtime behavior are consistent.

key_files.why: Central router for CDP JSON messages; maintains session<->BrowserContext mapping and is the entry point for dispatching all domain commands and emitting events.

Implements Page domain semantics (navigation, lifecycle events, scripts on new document, dialogs) where event ordering and script injection behavior are particularly sensitive for client compatibility.

Serializes network events (requestWillBeSent/responseReceived/loadingFinished/failed), manages extra headers and cookies, and interfaces with the HTTP transfer layer — changes here affect what clients observe about network traffic.
Node registry and DOM serialization are central to DOM/Accessibility/LP/DOM domain responses and search lifecycles; ID mappings here are relied on by many domains.
Test harness that simulates real websocket framing and runs Server.Client; modifications to CDP framing or send/receive semantics must keep tests updated to validate behavior.

construct_relationships: CDP.handleMessage (CDP.zig) parses incoming JSON and calls domain.processMessage in files under src/cdp/domains/* to implement command semantics.

Domain handlers (page/network/dom/fetch/etc.) call into BrowserContext/Page/Network/Cookie.Jar/Inspector constructs in the runtime layer to perform actual operations and then use CDP to emit events back to the client.
Node.zig registry is used by DOM, Accessibility, LP, and Page domains to register nodes, resolve backendNodeId, perform searches, and serialize nodes for responses and events.

Network domain serializes Transfer objects and emits Network.requestWillBeSent/responseReceived/loadingFinished; Fetch domain can pause Transfers via InterceptState and resume/fulfill/modify via Transfer APIs.
Page domain manages scripts_on_new_document and isolated worlds; addScriptToEvaluateOnNewDocument updates BrowserContext storage that Page consults during navigation and execution context creation.

Testing harness (testing.zig) creates a Server.Client over a socketpair and reads/parses WebSocket frames to validate what CDP would send to a real client, exercising the same framing/partial-read behavior as production.
notable_relationships: This subtree depends heavily on runtime constructs in src/browser (BrowserContext, Page, Inspector), src/network (Transfer, http types, Cookie.Jar) and the mcp Server.Client for WebSocket framing; edits here often require corresponding changes in those folders.
Event ordering and payload shapes must remain compatible with external DevTools tools (Puppeteer/Playwright); changing emission timing or field shapes in these handlers can break third-party tooling.
The test harness uses POSIX socketpair and low-level framing semantics — platform differences or changes to Server.Client framing require updates to testing.zig and unit tests.
CDP type/interface changes (e.g., switching Command signatures) propagate to all domain handlers and unit tests; keep synchronized with top-level CDP types and build targets.
recommendations: When editing routing or domain signatures, update CDP.Command concrete types and adjust all domain.processMessage signatures and call sites consistently to avoid compile/runtime mismatches.

Preserve DevTools expected event ordering semantics (especially Page lifecycle events) — changing navigation, load, or lifecycle hooks requires verifying client-visible event sequences with integration tests or a Puppeteer smoke test.

If modifying network serialization or cookie handling, ensure Transfer serialization includes Cookie and extra HTTP headers and run network-related unit tests and any end-to-end flows that inspect Network.* events.

Changes to script-on-new-document behavior must update BrowserContext storage and ensure Page navigation/isolated-world creation consults that store; check domain tests that assume scripts are injected on new documents.
Modify proxy or network teardown code with awareness of the refactored proxy API (e.g., changeProxy(null)); ensure BrowserContext teardown does not rely on removed methods.
Whenever modifying CDP framing, payload encoding, or sendJSON paths, run the socketpair-based CDP unit tests (src/cdp/testing.zig) and full make test to catch framing/serialization regressions.
Respect AX semantics: changes to AX serialization should use canonical Element semantics (isDisabled/etc.) to avoid accessibility mismatches reported by clients.
After changes, run repository formatting and tests (zig build fmt, make test) and exercise an end-to-end CDP client (e.g., Puppeteer demo) where possible to validate behavioral compatibility.

src.description: The src folder contains the in-process browser runtime and top-level application wiring for Lightpanda. It centralizes initialization/teardown (App), networking, CDP/server surfaces, page/frame lifecycles, DOM/WebAPI implementations, V8 integration and script/module loading, and a set of test fixtures and helpers. This subtree is the authoritative runtime: changes here must preserve lifecycle ordering, memory ownership, protocol compatibility and deterministic teardown because many external clients and tests depend on stable behavior.
key_behaviors: Global startup/shutdown: App creates and owns global subsystems (network, telemetry, arenas, V8 snapshot), and propagates that context into lower-layer modules.
Server/CDP accept loop: TCP/HTTP handling upgrades connections to WebSocket/CDP sessions, enforces request limits and concurrent-connection quotas, and routes messages to session/page handlers.

Page lifecycle orchestration: Session/Page create and destroy pages and frames, manage per-page arenas and V8 contexts, schedule navigation/networking, parse HTML, and run macrotasks/microtasks via Runner.
Network + cache + robots pipeline: HttpClient and network layer queue requests, consult cache and robots rules, perform libcurl transfers, and deliver Response objects back to Page/ScriptManager/XHR.
Script/module loading and V8 integration: ScriptManager and js.{Env,Context,bridge} resolve modules, download scripts, prevent reentrancy during evaluation, and map Zig-native APIs into V8.

DOM tooling and interaction: SemanticTree, Interactive/Form collectors, StyleManager and actions implement serialization, accessibility-like trees, visibility/pointer-events checks and emulate user input events for CDP/MCP.

insights.title: App must propagate itself into subsystems that rely on shared arenas
insights.problem: A subsystem (Network) required the App pointer to access the arena_pool during cache lookups, but App did not pass itself during initialization, causing runtime failures.
insights.root_cause: Order-of-initialization/ownership mismatch: some modules expect App-owned resources to be reachable via back-pointer rather than passed explicitly.
insights.solution: Ensure App is passed into or made available to subsystems that depend on App-owned resources during their init lifecycle; preserve init/deinit ordering so subsystems do not access freed resources during teardown.

insights.title: Arena allocator lifetimes must be explicit and thread-safe
insights.problem: Callers sometimes assumed transient arena-backed slices outlived callers; race/ownership issues and double-free/leak potential arose when arenas were reset or released incorrectly across threads.
insights.root_cause: Arenas are pooled and reused with a mutex but some call sites relied on implicit lifetimes or performed release in incorrect sequences (including releasing un-acquired refs on events).
insights.solution: Treat ArenaPool entries as explicitly acquired/released; respect mutex semantics and debug tracking; callers must not assume allocator-backed slices survive beyond the documented arena scope and must not release references they didn't acquire.

insights.title: Response abstraction and transport polymorphism must be respected
insights.problem: Refactors introduced a Response wrapper and moved transport (websocket vs curl Transfer) into Connection.transport; older code cast Transfer from connection private fields and accessed Transfer internals causing crashes or incorrect behavior.
insights.root_cause: Assumptions about a single concrete Transfer type stored in per-connection private pointers persisted after architectural changes that made transport polymorphic.
insights.solution: Use the Response abstraction and the connection.transport discriminant to access transport-specific data safely; avoid Transfer-specific casts unless transport is verified, and enforce a single client-level max response-size path.

insights.title: Timeouts must account for background browser activity
insights.problem: Server/client wait loops timed out even when the browser had active background work (HTTP requests/macrotasks), causing spurious disconnects or premature inactivity handling.
insights.root_cause: Wait/tick logic duplicated across Server and Runner and used different heuristics for 'busy' detection and timeout resets.
insights.solution: Use a single authoritative wait/tick policy (Runner._wait/_tick) that considers network and macrotask activity as work and ensures Server wait loops integrate that policy to avoid premature timeouts.

insights.title: Finalizer ordering and identity tracking must be centralized at Session scope
insights.problem: V8 finalizers ran against partially- or already-finalized objects because finalizer callbacks and identity tracking were scattered across identity/context scopes.
insights.root_cause: Finalizer semantics mixed fallback deinit logic with V8-driven callbacks and stored finalizers in identity-level storage; cross-context/IsolatedWorld sharing created unsafe ordering during teardown.
insights.solution: Centralize finalizer callbacks, globals and temps at Session scope and use explicit reference-counting semantics so teardown can deterministically clear identity state before running V8 finalizers.

insights.title: Synchronous returns from cache can cause reentrancy in ScriptManager
insights.problem: When a cached script was returned synchronously, evaluate() could run while addFromElement was still mutating ScriptManager state, breaching ordering/invariant guards like is_evaluating.
insights.root_cause: ScriptManager assumed downloads were asynchronous; cached-path shortcut invoked callbacks without the same scheduling constraints.
insights.solution: Guard against synchronous evaluation paths by deferring evaluation to a safe point (or ensuring state is fully configured before calling evaluate) and adapt callback signatures to the Response abstraction so script-loading paths behave uniformly.

insights.title: Inconsistent CLI flag naming and missing cache/fs options cause UX and runtime divergence
insights.problem: Cache directory flag names were inconsistent (--cache-dir vs --cache_dir), ws_max_concurrent wasn't controllable, and cache path configuration was missing or ambiguous.
insights.root_cause: Alias naming and help text generation were out of sync with parsing code and new options were added without full CLI surface changes.
insights.solution: Normalize flag names across parsing/help generation and ensure configuration accessors expose derived values for consumers (server, http client) so that runtime features (websocket concurrency, cache directory) are consistently respected.

key_files.why: Top-level owner of global subsystems (network, telemetry, arena pool, snapshot); changes affect init/deinit ordering and resource propagation across the runtime.
Entrypoint for TCP/HTTP/CDP accept loop and connection lifecycle; enforces request limits, upgrades to WebSocket/CDP sessions and interacts with Runner timeout semantics.
Manages per-browser session lifetime, V8 globals/finalizers, origin sharing and page creation/removal; central to memory/identity and finalizer invariants.
Represents per-frame browsing context, coordinates navigation, DOM lifecycle, integrates with ScriptManager and HttpClient and is sensitive to allocator and response handling semantics.
Implements script/module download and evaluation semantics, ordering guarantees and interacts tightly with HttpClient and V8 (is_evaluating guard and import resolution).

construct_relationships: App initializes and owns ArenaPool, Network/HttpClient, Telemetry and V8 snapshot; it must be provided to Server and Session so subsystems can access global resources.
Server accepts TCP connections and, after WebSocket upgrade, maps sessions to Session/Page lifecycle managers via MCP/CDP handlers.
Session creates Pages and coordinates V8 Env/Context creation; Context instances map v8::Context to Zig Context and rely on Session-level identity/finalizer tracking.

Page uses HttpClient for navigation and resource loads, StyleManager/Interactive/Form collectors for visibility and interactivity queries, ScriptManager to schedule and evaluate scripts, and Runner to tick/poll until navigation conditions are met.
ScriptManager and HttpClient interact via queued requests and Response callbacks; Response abstraction replaces Transfer-specific access and requires transport checks in network callbacks.

ArenaPool provides transient allocators to Page/Session/DOM helpers; caller code must acquire/release arenas, and many DOM serializers (SemanticTree, forms, interactive) return arena-backed slices that borrow allocator memory.
notable_relationships: The src/network layer (libcurl-backed) is a shared dependency for Page/ScriptManager/Server (CDP websockets), so changes to connection/transport models affect both navigation and protocol sessions.

js.{Env,Context,bridge} form the V8 integration backbone and depend on snapshot creation and build-time snapshot.bin; changes may require snapshot regeneration (main_snapshot_creator) and build flags in build.zig.

CDP/MCP protocol modules (src/mcp, src/cdp) rely on stable node/backend id registration performed by SemanticTree/interactive/forms to target DOM operations; regressions break external clients (Playwright/Puppeteer) and tests.
HTML parsing and DOM construction rely on the html5ever Rust crate (outside Zig sources); ABI/serialization assumptions between Rust and Zig must be preserved when modifying DOM-related structures.
Tests and example fixtures (browser/tests/*.html) are used by unit and E2E suites; many CI jobs and demo harnesses depend on these artifacts and the Makefile/CI build matrix for verification.
recommendations: Preserve explicit init/deinit ordering: App -> Network/HttpClient -> Session/Env -> Pages; ensure subsystems are not accessed after deinit.
When changing networking or response handling, update all call sites that relied on Transfer internals to use the Response abstraction and verify transport before casting.
Respect ArenaPool acquire/release semantics and allocator lifetimes: any API returning arena-backed slices must document allocator ownership and callers must not outlive the arena.
Maintain centralized finalizer/identity behavior at Session scope; avoid scattering finalizer semantics across Context/Identity to prevent double-finalize or use-after-free during teardown.
Ensure Runner and Server share the same 'busy' and timeout semantics to prevent premature timeouts; test paths that exercise background network/macrotasks.
When modifying CLI/config accessors, keep flag names and help text consistent and exercise runtime paths that read those options (server, http client, cache dir and ws concurrency).

Run the full test suite and relevant integration tests after edits: zig build fmt, zig build test (unit), make end2end (demo-based E2E) and CDP/MCP protocol tests that exercise node registration, actions and XHR fixtures.
Be mindful of V8 snapshot and build-time dependencies: changes to js bridge/Env/Context or startup code may require running the snapshot_creator and adjusting build.zig flags.

..description: This folder is the Lightpanda product root: it contains the Zig build orchestration and the in-process browser runtime (App, server/CDP surface, page/frame runtime, V8 bindings, HTTP stack, DOM tooling, actions and tests). It defines the lifecycle, memory ownership and cross-language build/link requirements (V8, libcurl, native C libs, and a Rust html5ever crate) that must remain consistent to preserve protocol compatibility, deterministic teardown, and binary reproducibility.
key_behaviors: Global initialization and teardown: App constructs and owns global subsystems (network, arenas, telemetry, V8 snapshot) and propagates that context to lower layers.

Build and snapshot flow: build.zig composes native links (V8, curl, brotli, zlib, boringssl, nghttp2), generates a snapshot creator, and enforces formatting/compilation constraints used by CI and release builds.
CDP server lifecycle: Server accepts TCP/HTTP connections, enforces request/concurrency limits, upgrades to WebSocket/CDP sessions and routes messages to Browser/Session/Page handlers.

Page navigation & scripting: Page + Session + Runner coordinate navigation, network requests (HttpClient -> libcurl), HTML parsing, script download/evaluation (ScriptManager -> V8 Context/Env), and macrotask/microtask pumping.
Resource/identity/finalizer management: Session/Context/Env centralize V8 identity, finalizers, TAOs and lifetime tracking to avoid unsafe finalization during teardown.

DOM tooling and user actions: SemanticTree, Interactive, Form, StyleManager and actions.zig produce serializable artifacts (semantic tree, interactive elements, forms) and emulate user interactions (click, fill, setChecked) used by CDP endpoints and tests.

insights.title: Version string must be URL-safe in crash reports
insights.problem: Version strings can contain characters (e.g., '+') that break query semantics when sent to crash servers.
insights.root_cause: Unencoded version string used verbatim in crash report query string.
insights.solution: Percent-encode or otherwise sanitize the version string before embedding in URLs; ensure build pipeline exposes encoded version to runtime.

insights.title: Support lightweight compile-only check
insights.problem: CI and local workflows sometimes need a fast verification that the codebase compiles without building full release artifacts or snapshots.
insights.root_cause: Default build graph composes heavy native links and snapshot creation, making fast compile checks expensive.
insights.solution: Provide and respect a compilation-only 'check' build step in build.zig so tooling can run quick compile verifications.

insights.title: App must propagate itself to subsystems that need arena/context access
insights.problem: Network (and other subsystems) required access to App-owned resources (e.g., arena_pool) but were not passed the App pointer, causing runtime errors.
insights.root_cause: Implicit assumptions about global availability of App-owned resources rather than explicit ownership propagation.
insights.solution: Ensure App is explicitly provided to subsystems at init so they can use App-managed arenas and lifecycle hooks.

insights.title: ArenaPool reuse and debug tracking must be thread-safe
insights.problem: Mismanagement of pooled arenas or improper reset semantics can leak memory or double-free in debug builds.
insights.root_cause: Concurrent acquire/release across threads and reset work done while holding locks inconsistently.
insights.solution: Keep minimal lock-time work; perform resets outside locks where possible; maintain debug acquire/release counters to detect leaks/double-frees.

insights.title: Do not rely on Transfer-private pointer shape; use Response wrapper consistently
insights.problem: Code assumed Transfer internals via Connection.private pointer; websocket transport and other refactors made these casts unsafe and inconsistent.
insights.root_cause: Scattered direct usage of Transfer-specific fields that changed shape after transport abstraction updates.
insights.solution: Centralize network callbacks around the Response abstraction and check Connection.transport before any transfer-specific casts; ensure client-level max_response_size is enforced consistently.

insights.title: Normalize CLI flag names and expose cache/ws limits
insights.problem: Inconsistent flag naming (--cache-dir vs --cache_dir) and missing runtime knobs (ws_max_concurrent, --http-cache-dir) caused confusing UX and inability to tune runtime behavior.
insights.root_cause: CLI help and parsing used inconsistent naming and omitted configuration options required by network/server subsystems.
insights.solution: Treat configuration names as canonical across modules; ensure Config exposes ws_max_concurrent, cache_dir, and derived defaults consumed by Server and HttpClient.

insights.title: Finalize ordering and identity must be centralized
insights.problem: V8 finalizers ran against already-cleared identity data or used mixed fallback deinit semantics, causing double-finalize or unsafe operations during teardown.
insights.root_cause: Finalizer callbacks and identity tracking were scattered across Identity/Context levels and mixed implicit fallback _deinit semantics.
insights.solution: Centralize finalizer callbacks and temp/global tracking at Session scope and use explicit reference-counted finalizer semantics; clear identity before forcing finalizers.

insights.title: Runner wait/timeouts must consider background work
insights.problem: Wait loops timed out even when the browser had active background work (HTTP requests/macrotasks), leading to premature errors.
insights.root_cause: Timeout logic duplicated at multiple levels and not aware of active work or CDP wakeups.
insights.solution: Consolidate timeout and busy-detection in the Runner so wait/tick semantics account for in-flight work and CDP wakeups.

insights.title: Guard against synchronous evaluation on cached script returns
insights.problem: Cached script data returned synchronously could trigger evaluate() while addFromElement was mid-setup, causing reentrancy and inconsistent evaluation guards.
insights.root_cause: ScriptManager assumed asynchronous network callbacks; cache hits violated that assumption.
insights.solution: Ensure addFromElement establishes its state/is_evaluating guard before any path can synchronously call evaluate(); adapt callbacks to Response abstraction.

insights.title: Accurate reference acquire/release and event ordering
insights.problem: Code released event references that were never acquired and dispatched DOM events in incorrect order (e.g., change/input vs click), producing incorrect behavior for event listeners.
insights.root_cause: Event dispatch and refcount patterns were implemented inconsistently across input dispatching code.
insights.solution: Ensure event acquire/release symmetry, focus semantics before fill, and DOM event ordering matches browser behavior (input/change vs click ordering).

key_files.why: Central build orchestration: validates Zig toolchain, links native deps (V8/libcurl/etc.), creates snapshot/binaries and enforces format/check steps that CI and reproducible builds depend on.
Application root that constructs and owns global subsystems (network, arena pool, telemetry, V8 snapshot) and is the single source of runtime lifetimes and propagation to lower layers.
TCP/HTTP/CDP acceptor and connection manager: enforces request/size/concurrency limits and upgrades to WebSocket/CDP sessions driving external client interactions.
Encapsulates script loading semantics and coordinates HTTP fetches, import resolution and V8 evaluation order—changes here affect page execution ordering and reentrancy behavior.
High-level HTTP client used across Pages and ScriptManager; enforces caching, robots handling, interception, connection tracking and response sizing—critical to network correctness.

construct_relationships: App -> Network: App initializes and passes its pointer into Network so that network components can access App-managed arena_pool and lifecycle hooks.

Server -> App/Config -> Client/Session/Page: Server uses App and Config to accept TCP connections, enforce concurrency limits, and spawn per-connection Client workers that operate on Session/Page objects.

Page -> ScriptManager & HttpClient: Page delegates script downloads and execution to ScriptManager which uses HttpClient to fetch script resources; ScriptManager then evaluates scripts in V8 Contexts created by Env/Context.

Session -> Env/Context: Session creates and manages V8 Isolates/Contexts (Env/Context), centralizing identity, globals, finalizers and TAO lifetimes to coordinate cross-context references and teardown ordering.
Runner -> HttpClient & Page: Runner polls HttpClient and advances Page macrotasks/microtasks, implementing wait/tick semantics and CDP wakeups; Runner drives navigation readiness and timeout logic.
notable_relationships: build.zig <-> native toolchain & CI: build.zig ties the code to native prebuilt V8 artifacts, libcurl and Rust html5ever; changes to build options affect CI, Docker images and release snapshot generation.

src/html5ever (Rust crate) <-> Zig runtime: HTML parsing is implemented in a Rust crate built via Cargo during the Zig build; local edits that change HTML parse interfaces may require coordinated Rust/Cargo builds.

CDP compatibility with external clients: Server, SemanticTree, Interactive and forms modules produce shapes/IDs expected by CDP clients (Playwright/Puppeteer); changing serialized shapes or backendNodeId registration affects external tooling.

Runtime config & CLI: Config schema (flags and derived values) is consumed widely (Server, HttpClient, telemetry); renaming flags or changing defaults must be kept consistent across modules and CI test invocations.

V8 snapshot lifecycle: Snapshot generation (via snapshot creator) and Env initialization are coupled; modifying JS bridge/boot code requires snapshot regeneration for release builds to remain consistent.
recommendations: Respect and preserve explicit ownership and teardown ordering: App -> Session -> Env/Context -> Page must keep deterministic finalizer ordering; ensure identities are cleared before forcing V8 finalizers.

When changing networking code, use the Response abstraction consistently and avoid direct Transfer/Connection.private assumptions; enforce client-level max_response_size and check Connection.transport before casts.

Any edits touching V8 bindings, JS bridge, Context or Env require regenerating snapshots for release builds and running V8-related tests; update build.zig snapshot inputs and CI artifacts accordingly.
Maintain thread-safety of ArenaPool and server concurrency counters; verify acquire/release symmetry and run unit tests that exercise concurrent acquire/release and client connection limits.
Keep CLI/config names canonical: update Config accessors and propagate changes to Server and tests; ensure flag name normalization to avoid parsing discrepancies used in CI and demo tooling.
Validate DOM/event behavior against tests: changes to actions, event dispatch or reference counting must be covered by browser tests (src/browser/tests/*) and MCP/CDP integration checks (end-to-end).

Run formatting and compilation checks via build.zig (default format check) and run unit tests (zig build test / make test) and end-to-end flows (make end2end) when modifying protocol surfaces (CDP, semantic tree, interactive lists).

When modifying serialization shapes (SemanticTree, Interactive, forms), ensure backendNodeId assignment and JSON shapes remain compatible with CDP consumers; run protocol/client interoperability tests after edits.
If adding or renaming build/link options or native deps, update build.zig and CI/Dockerfile to maintain reproducible release pipelines and ensure Cargo/Rust build steps for html5ever remain intact.
